An $S$-point of a scheme $X$ is a morphism $\phi: T \rightarrow X$ where $T$ is a scheme over a base scheme $S$, representing a point in the context of the base.
A commutative diagram is a graphical representation of mathematical relationships where all paths with the same start and endpoints yield the same result, ensuring consistency in the mappings.
A locally ringed space is a topological space $X$ equipped with a sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_{X}$ such that for each point $p \in X$, the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{X,p}$ is a local ring.

A morphism from $(X,\mathcal{O}_{X})$ to $(Y,\mathcal{O}_{Y})$ consists of a continuous map $f: X \to Y$ and a morphism of sheaves $\phi: \mathcal{O}_{Y} \to \mathcal{O}_{X}$ such that the induced ring homomorphism on stalks is a local homomorphism.

Concept: Tangent Vectors in Locally Ringed Spaces
Explanation: For a locally ringed space $X$, the space of tangent vectors at a point $p$ is defined as the dual vector space $({\mathfrak{m}_{p}/{\mathfrak{m}_{p}^{2}}})^{*}$, where $k$ is the residue field $\mathcal{O}_{X,p}/{\mathfrak{m}_{p}}$.

The construction of schemes involves the forgetful functor that assigns to each locally ringed space $(X,\mathcal{O}_{X})$ the ring $\mathcal{O}_{X}(X)$, which is adjoint to the prime spectrum functor taking a ring $R$ to the locally ringed space $Spec(R)$.
The prime spectrum ${Spec}{(R)}$ of a commutative ring $R$ is defined as the set of all proper prime ideals of $R$.
A subset $F$ of a topological space $X$ is reducible if it can be expressed as a union $F = F_{1} \cup F_{2}$ of two closed proper subsets $F_{1}$ and $F_{2}$ of $F$.
A subset of a topological space is irreducible (or *hyperconnected*) if it is not reducible.

Multiplicative units of a ring are the elements that have a multiplicative inverse. In this case, the $\mathbb{Z}$-points of ${Spec}R$ correspond to the multiplicative units of $\mathbb{Z}$, which are $1$ and $-1$.

A group scheme is a scheme that has a group structure, meaning it has a multiplication operation that is compatible with the scheme structure. The scheme ${\mathbb{G}}_{m}$ is an example of a group scheme representing multiplicative units.

The fiber of a morphism at a point is the preimage of that point under the morphism, which in this case relates to the structure of the scheme over a point in ${Spec}{\mathbb{Z}}$. The fiber is represented as ${{Spec}{R 	imes {{Spec}k}}}{(x)}$.

Hilbert modular varieties are geometric objects that parameterize abelian varieties with additional structure, often studied in the context of number theory and algebraic geometry. The article mentions their behavior in relation to families of schemes in different characteristics.

A scheme is a locally ringed space $(X,\mathcal{O}_{X})$ that has an open cover ${\{ U_{\alpha}\}}_{\alpha \in I}$ such that each open set $U_{\alpha}$, along with its restriction sheaf ${\mathcal{O}_{X}|}_{U_{\alpha}}$, is an affine scheme.
A scheme over a scheme $Y$ is a scheme $X$ together with a morphism of schemes $X \longrightarrow Y$, called the structure morphism of $X$.
An $S$-point on a scheme $X$ is a morphism $S \rightarrow X$. The functor $S \mapsto {Hom}(S,X)$ is called the functor of points on $X$.
A generic point is a point in the underlying topological space of a scheme that corresponds to an irreducible closed subset, whose closure is the whole subset.
A scheme $X$ is a separated scheme if the morphism $d: X \rightarrow X \times_{Spec \mathbb{Z}} X$ induced by the identity maps is a closed immersion.
The diagonal morphism $d: X \rightarrow X \times_{Y} X$ is used to define the property of a scheme being separated over a base scheme $Y$.

The singular set of a variety $X$ is defined as the set of singular points, which forms a proper subvariety of $X$. A subvariety $Y$ of $X$ is contained in the singular set if and only if its local ring $\mathcal{O}_{Y}$ is not regular.
The prime spectrum of a ring $R$, denoted ${Spec}(R)$, is the set of all prime ideals of the ring $R$.
A topological space is quasi-compact if every open cover has a finite subcover. In this proof, it is shown that ${Spec}(R)$ satisfies this property.
An open cover of a space is a collection of open sets whose union contains the space. In this proof, the collection $\{ U_{\lambda} \}_{\lambda \in \Lambda}$ is an open cover for ${Spec}(R)$.
A finite subcover is a finite selection of sets from an open cover that still covers the entire space. The proof demonstrates that ${Spec}(R)$ has such a finite subcover.
For an ideal $I$, $V(I)$ is the set of all prime ideals of $R$ that contain $I$. It is used in the context of defining open sets in the prime spectrum.

A commutative ring with identity is a ring $R$ in which the multiplication is commutative, there exists a multiplicative identity element (usually denoted as 1), and the ring satisfies the ring axioms.
A proper prime ideal is a prime ideal that is not equal to the entire ring $R$. It is an ideal $P$ such that if $ab \in P$, then either $a \in P$ or $b \in P$.

An invertible sheaf $\mathfrak{L}$ on a scheme $X$ is called *ample* if for any coherent sheaf $\mathfrak{F}$, $\mathfrak{F} \otimes \mathfrak{L}^{n}$ is generated by global sections for sufficiently large $n$.
An invertible sheaf is ample if and only if $\mathfrak{L}^{m}$ is very ample for some $m$; this is often taken as the definition of ample.

The Segre map is an embedding $s:\mathbb{P}^{n} \times \mathbb{P}^{m} \rightarrow \mathbb{P}^{nm + n + m}$ that maps the product of two projective spaces into a larger projective space, allowing the product of two projective varieties to remain a projective variety.

Determinantal varieties are varieties defined as the common zero locus of the determinants of matrices, particularly those with rank constraints, such as the vanishing of minors in some space of matrices.

In projective geometry, a set of points is in general position if any $d + 2$ of them do not lie on a $d$-dimensional plane. For example, 4 points are in general position if no three of them are collinear.
Dually, a set of $d$-dimensional planes is in general position if no $d + 2$ of them meet at the same point, illustrating the dual relationship between points and planes in projective geometry.

A bivector is a two-dimensional analog to a vector, representing a two-dimensional directed quantity, visualized as an oriented plane-segment. It is constructed from two linearly independent vectors, $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$, using the exterior product: $\mathbf{B} = {\mathbf{a} \land \mathbf{b}}$. The orientation is determined by the order of the vectors in the product.

A Cartier divisor on a scheme $X$ is defined as a global section of the sheaf $\mathcal{K}^{*}/\mathcal{O}^{*}$, where $\mathcal{K}^{*}$ represents the multiplicative sheaf of meromorphic functions and $\mathcal{O}^{*}$ represents the multiplicative sheaf of invertible regular functions. It involves an open cover $U_{i}$ of $X$ and meromorphic functions $f_{i}$ such that the ratio $f_{i}/f_{j}$ belongs to $\mathcal{O}^{*}({U_{i} \cap U_{j}})$. Two Cartier divisors are considered the same if one open cover is a refinement of the other, or if the functions are modified by a unit in $\mathcal{O}_{*}$.

A sheaf $\mathfrak{L}$ of $\mathcal{O}_{X}$ modules is called invertible if there exists another sheaf $\mathfrak{L}^{\prime}$ such that $\mathfrak{L} \otimes \mathfrak{L}^{\prime} \cong \mathcal{O}_{X}$. It is equivalent to being locally free of rank 1.
A normal irreducible variety is a type of algebraic variety that is irreducible and satisfies the property of normality, meaning its local rings are integrally closed domains.

Codimension is a measure of the dimension of a subvariety relative to the ambient variety, specifically the difference between the dimension of the ambient variety and the dimension of the subvariety.
A discrete valuation ring is a type of local ring that is a principal ideal domain, which has a unique non-zero maximal ideal and is used in the study of valuation theory.

Serre's twisting theorem states that for a scheme $X$ and an ample invertible sheaf $\mathfrak{L}$ on $X$, the higher sheaf cohomology $H^{i}(\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathfrak{L}^{n})$ is trivial for any coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ and sufficiently large $n$.
If $k$ is algebraically closed, the Riemann-Roch theorem shows that on a curve $X$, any invertible sheaf of degree greater than or equal to twice the genus of $X$ is very ample.

A sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{X}$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ is called locally free if for each point $p \in X$, there exists an open neighborhood $U$ of $p$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_{U}$ is free as an $\mathcal{O}_{X}|_{U}$-module, or equivalently, the stalk $\mathcal{F}_{p}$ is free as a $\mathcal{O}_{X,p}$-module.
If the stalk $\mathcal{F}_{p}$ is of finite rank $n$, then the sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ is said to be of rank $n$.

The sheaf of meromorphic functions, denoted as \( \mathcal{K}_{X} \), is constructed from the localization of the sheaf of holomorphic functions on a ringed space \( (X, \mathcal{O}_{X}) \). It generalizes the notion of meromorphic functions on complex manifolds and schemes, providing a framework for studying function fields in birational geometry.

Sheafification is the process of associating a sheaf to a presheaf, ensuring that it satisfies the sheaf axioms. The sheaf of meromorphic functions \( \mathcal{K}_{X} \) is the sheafification of the presheaf constructed from localizations.
The degree of a divisor $D$ is defined as $\deg{(D)} = \sum n_{p}$.
The Néron-Severi group of a surface $S$, denoted by $NS(S)$, is the group of Weil divisors on $S$ modulo algebraic equivalence of divisors.
Weil divisors are formal sums of codimension one subvarieties on an algebraic variety, used in the context of the Néron-Severi group.
Algebraic equivalence is a relation between divisors that identifies them if they can be connected by a family of algebraic varieties.
The Néron-Severi group of a surface is a finitely generated abelian group, meaning it can be expressed as a finite direct sum of cyclic groups.
The intersection divisor of a quartic curve $C$ and a line $L$ is expressed as $({L \cdot C}) = {P_{1} + P_{2} + P_{3} + P_{4}}$, where $P_{i}$ are intersection points in $C(K)$.
A quartic curve $C$ is a non-singular curve defined by a polynomial of degree 4 in the plane.
Tangency occurs when a line $L$ is tangent to a quartic curve $C$, resulting in two intersection points being the same, e.g., $P_{1} = P_{2}$.

A line $L$ is bitangent to a quartic curve $C$ if it intersects at two pairs of points, $P_{1} = P_{2}$ and $P_{3} = P_{4}$, with $P_{1} 
eq P_{3}$.

A flex is a point $P_{1}$ on a quartic curve $C$ where a line $L$ intersects $C$ at exactly three points, $P_{1} = P_{2} = P_{3} 
eq P_{4}$.
A hyperflex is a point where a line $L$ intersects a quartic curve $C$ at exactly one point, with all intersection points being the same, $P_{1} = P_{2} = P_{3} = P_{4}$.
An algebraic family of effective divisors on a surface $X$ parametrized by a non-singular curve $T$ is defined as an effective Cartier divisor $\mathcal{D}$ on $X \times T$ which is flat over $T$.

For an algebraic family of effective divisors parametrized by a non-singular curve $T$, two closed points $P,Q \in T$ correspond to divisors $D_{P},D_{Q}$ that are said to be prealgebraically equivalent.

Two (Weil) divisors $D,D^{\prime}$ on $X$ are algebraically equivalent if there exists a finite sequence $D = {D_{0},D_{1},\ldots},D_{n} = D^{\prime}$ such that each pair $D_{i},D_{i + 1}$ is prealgebraically equivalent for all $0 \leq i < n$.

A Weil divisor is an element of the abelian group ${Div}(X)$ generated by prime divisors on a noetherian integral separated scheme $X$. It can be expressed as ${\mathcal{W} = \sum{n_{Y}Y}}$, where $n_{Y}$ are integers and only finitely many are non-zero.
A divisor is said to be effective if $n_{Y} \geq 0$ for all prime divisors $Y$.

The Kodaira-Itaka dimension of a line bundle $L$ on a projective algebraic variety $X$ is defined as the supremum of the dimensions of the image of $X$ under the map $\varphi_{|{mL}|}$ associated with the linear system $|{mL}|$, for positive integers $m$. It is given by the formula ${{\kappa{(L)}} = {\sup\limits_{m \in {\mathbb{N}}}{\{{\dim{\varphi_{|{mL}|}{(X)}}}\}}}}$.
The Kodaira dimension of a projective algebraic variety $X$ is the Kodaira-Itaka dimension of its canonical bundle $K_{X}$, denoted as $\kappa(K_{X})$.
A line bundle $L$ is called big if there exists some positive integer $m$ such that the dimension of the image $\dim{\varphi_{|{mL}|}{(X)}}$ equals $\dim X$, which implies $\kappa(L) = \dim X$.
A projective algebraic variety $X$ is said to be of general type if its Kodaira dimension $\kappa(X)$ equals the dimension of $X$, specifically when $\kappa(X) = \kappa(K_{X}) = \dim X$.
A presheaf is a contravariant functor from a category of open sets (basis of a topology) to another category, which assigns to each open set an object and to each inclusion of open sets a morphism.
A basis of a topology on a set $X$ is a collection of open sets such that every open set in the topology can be expressed as a union of sets from this collection.

A contravariant functor is a mapping between categories that reverses the direction of morphisms, meaning if there is a morphism $f: A 	o B$ in the first category, then the functor maps it to a morphism $F(B) 	o F(A)$ in the second category.

A sheaf of abelian groups $\mathcal{F}$ over a topological space $X$ is a mathematical structure that associates to each open set $U \subset X$ an abelian group $\mathcal{F}(U)$, satisfying certain gluing and locality conditions.

A subsheaf $\mathcal{G}$ of a sheaf of abelian groups $\mathcal{F}$ is defined such that for every open set $U \subset X$, $\mathcal{G}(U)$ is a subgroup of $\mathcal{F}(U)$, and the restrictions on $\mathcal{G}$ are those of $\mathcal{F}$.

The quotient topology on the sheaf $\mathcal{F}/\mathcal{G}$ is defined by taking the topology induced by the quotient of the stalks $\mathcal{F}_{x}/\mathcal{G}_{x}$, where $\mathcal{G}$ is a subsheaf of $\mathcal{F}$.

The sheaf of germs of meromorphic functions $M^{*}$ consists of equivalence classes of meromorphic functions defined on open sets of a complex manifold, where two functions are equivalent if they agree on some neighborhood.
The sheaf $M^{*}/O^{*}$ represents the sheaf of divisors, which encodes information about the zeros and poles of meromorphic functions on a complex manifold.

A subbundle \( \mathcal{E}^{\prime} \) of a vector bundle \( \mathcal{E} \) is a subset such that the restriction of the projection map \( \pi \) to \( \mathcal{E}^{\prime} \) is also a vector bundle, and for each point \( p \in M \), the fibre \( \pi|_{\mathcal{E}^{\prime}}^{-1}(p) \) is a vector subspace of the fibre \( \mathcal{E}_{p} = \pi^{-1}(p) \).

The functor $\iota: S_{T} \rightarrow P_{T}$ has a left adjoint $\sharp$, which establishes a correspondence between the morphisms in the categories of sheaves and presheaves, characterized by the hom-set isomorphism ${Hom}_{S_{T}}(G^{\sharp},F) \cong {Hom}_{P_{T}}(G,{\iota F})$.
The direct image functor $f_{*}:\mathbf{Sh}(X) \rightarrow \mathbf{Sh}(Y)$ sends a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$ to its direct image $f_{*}\mathcal{F}$ on $Y$, preserving the structure of sheaves.

A morphism of sheaves $g: \mathcal{F} \rightarrow \mathcal{G}$ is a structure-preserving map between two sheaves, which induces a morphism $f_{*}g: f_{*}\mathcal{F} \rightarrow f_{*}\mathcal{G}$ under the direct image functor.

In the context of sheaves, exactness refers to the condition that the left-hand map is injective and the two right-hand maps agree on its image when $C$ is the category of sets, or that the kernel conditions hold when $C$ is an abelian category.
A site is a category that captures the necessary features of a topological space required to form sheaves, allowing for the definition of sheaves in a more general context.

The $l$-adic étale cohomology of a scheme $X$ over a field $k$ is defined as $H_{	ext{ét}}^{i}(X,{\mathbb{Q}}_{l}) = {\mathbb{Q}}_{l} \otimes_{{\mathbb{Z}}_{l}}{\lim\limits_{\leftarrow}_{n}{(R^{i}\Gamma)({\mathbb{Z}}/l^{n}{\mathbb{Z}})}}$, where $R^{i}$ denotes the $i$-th right-derived functor.
Étale cohomology is an appropriate generalization of de Rham cohomology on a complex manifold, particularly when $l$ is not equal to the characteristic of $k$.
On a scheme of dimension $n$, the cohomology groups $H^{i}$ vanish for $i > 2n$, and there exists a version of Poincaré duality.

The Čech cohomology ${\check{H}}^{i}(X,\mathcal{F})$ is calculated using an open cover $\{ U_{i} \}$ of $X$ and is defined through a chain complex constructed from the sheaf sections over intersections of the open sets.

A cover is *sufficiently fine* if ${H^{i}(U_{j},\mathcal{F})} = 0$ for all $i > 0$, for every $j$ and for every sheaf $\mathcal{F}$, allowing for the computation of sheaf cohomology via finite processes.
Surjective étale morphisms are the appropriate generalization of covering spaces in the algebraic category.
The algebraic fundamental group is defined as an inverse limit of automorphism groups of finite étale morphisms.
A geometric point of a scheme $X$ is a point that is considered in the context of the étale fundamental group construction.
A morphism is unramified if it has no infinitesimal extensions, meaning that the relative sheaf of differentials vanishes.
In topology, a local homeomorphism is a function that is a homeomorphism when restricted to a neighborhood of each point.
Tangent spaces are vector spaces that approximate the local behavior of a variety at a point, and an étale morphism induces an isomorphism on these spaces.

Serre duality states that on certain schemes $X$ of dimension $n$, there is a natural perfect pairing between $Ext^{i}(\mathcal{F},\omega)$ and $H^{n - i}(X,\mathcal{F})$, where $\mathcal{F}$ is a coherent sheaf and $\omega$ is the dualizing sheaf.
A perfect pairing is a natural map that induces an isomorphism between $Ext^{i}(\mathcal{F},\omega)$ and $Hom(H^{n - i}(X,\mathcal{F}),k)$.
The dualizing sheaf $\omega$ plays a crucial role in Serre duality, especially when $X$ is nonsingular or Cohen-Macaulay, where it is represented as $\bigwedge^{n}\Omega$.
A scheme is Cohen-Macaulay if it has nice properties regarding its local rings, which allows for the simplification of the dualizing sheaf in the context of Serre duality.
Motivic cohomology is a cohomology theory defined for schemes and algebraic varieties, adapting concepts from topology to the algebraic setting.
Weight zero motivic cohomology corresponds to the Chow groups, which are the groups of codimension i subvarieties of a scheme modulo rational equivalence.
Higher Chow groups, denoted as \( CH^{q}(X,n) \), are defined using algebraic cycles on \( X 	imes \Delta^{n} \) and provide an equivalent definition of motivic cohomology.
Motivic cohomology in higher weights corresponds to Bloch's higher Chow groups and rationally coincides with graded pieces of higher algebraic K-theory \( K_{i}(X) \).
The motivic cohomology groups are defined as the hypercohomology of complexes of sheaves in the Zariski or Nisnevich topologies.

The Leray spectral sequence is a tool in algebraic geometry that relates the cohomology of a space $X$ to the cohomology of its image $Y$ under a morphism $f$. Specifically, for an affine morphism, it states that the higher direct images vanish, leading to the equality of cohomology groups: $H^{i}(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^{i}(Y, f_{*}\mathcal{F})$.

An affine morphism is a type of morphism between schemes where every point in the target has an affine neighborhood whose preimage is also affine. This property simplifies the study of the morphism's behavior.

The operation '$*$' defined on the set $G$ in a group is referred to as the *group operation* of $G$. It is often denoted simply by juxtaposition (i.e., $ab$) or by '$+$' in the case of abelian groups.
Group actions are a way to study groups by examining how they act on other mathematical objects, often revealing symmetries and properties of those objects.
Faltings' Theorem states that for a non-singular curve $C/K$ defined over a number field $K$ with genus $g \geq 2$, the set of $K$-rational points $C(K)$ is finite.

The Mordell-Weil theorem states that the group of $K$-rational points of an elliptic curve over a number field is finitely generated, meaning it can be expressed as a direct sum of a finite group and a free abelian group.
Mordell's conjecture posited that for a non-singular curve of genus $g \geq 2$, the set of $K$-rational points $C(K)$ cannot be infinite, which was proven by Faltings.
A variety $V$ is termed *soluble* in $K$ if the set of points $V{(K)}$ is not empty.

A variety $V$ satisfies the *Hasse condition* if it is locally soluble for all valuations $
u$ of the field $K$.
A quadric is a type of algebraic variety defined by a single quadratic equation, which satisfies the Hasse condition as shown by Minkowski and Hasse.
A global field is defined as either a global number field or a global function field.
A global number field is a finite extension of the rationals.
A global function field is a finite extension of the field of rational functions $k(t)$ of a finite field $k$.
Hypertext Markup Language (HTML) is an application of SGML for creating documents to exchange over the Internet using the Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP).
TeX is a typesetting system often used for writing mathematical formulas, which can be parsed and rendered in HTML pages.
Superscripts and subscripts are formatting options in HTML that allow for the representation of powers and indices, but are insufficient for more complex mathematical expressions.
The radical sign is used to denote roots in mathematics, but HTML's representation is limited, especially for overlapping radicals.

The $q^{th}$-power Frobenius morphism is defined as $\phi: C \rightarrow C^{(q)}$ where $\phi([x_{0},\ldots,x_{N}]) = [x_{0}^{q},\ldots,x_{N}^{q}]$. It transforms points on the curve $C$ into points on the curve $C^{(q)}$.
Weil conjectured that the zeta function should be a rational function, meaning it can be expressed as the ratio of two polynomials.
Weil proposed that the zeta function should split into polynomial parts with integral coefficients, indicating a structured relationship between the function's roots and the geometry of the variety.
The conjecture includes a condition on the roots of the zeta function, which should have a certain magnitude, reflecting geometric properties of the variety.

Weil conjectured that the degree of the zeta function is equal to the Betti number, which is a topological invariant that counts the maximum number of cuts that can be made without dividing a space into two separate pieces.

The logarithmic spiral is defined in polar coordinates by the equation $r = Ce^{k\varphi}$, where $C > 0$ and $k$ are constants. It is characterized by its property of maintaining a constant angle $\psi$ with any line emanating from the origin.

The arc length $s$ of the logarithmic spiral over an interval $[\varphi_{1}, \varphi_{2}]$ is given by the integral $s = \int_{\varphi_{1}}^{\varphi_{2}} \sqrt{r^{2} + \left( \frac{dr}{d\varphi} \right)^{2}} d\varphi$, which can be expressed in closed form for the logarithmic spiral.
The polar tangential angle $\psi$ is the constant angle between the radius vector and the tangent to the logarithmic spiral, related to the constant $k$ by the equation $k = \cot\psi$.

A vector-valued function assigns a vector to each point in its domain. In the case of the logarithmic spiral, the position vector is given by $\overset{\rightarrow}{r} = (Ce^{k\varphi}\cos\varphi, Ce^{k\varphi}\sin\varphi)$, representing the curve in Cartesian coordinates.
An Archimedean spiral is defined by the polar equation $r = {a\theta^{1/t}}$, where $a$ is a real number, $r$ is the radial distance, $	heta$ is the angle, and $t$ is a constant.
The curvature of an Archimedean spiral is given by the formula $\frac{t\theta^{1 - {1/t}}{({{t^{2}\theta^{2}} + t + 1})}}{a{({{t^{2}\theta^{2}} + 1})}^{3/2}}$.

The torsion of a curve, denoted as \( \tau(t) \), measures the rate at which the osculating plane of the curve rotates about the tangent vector. It is defined as \( \tau(t) = \frac{{B(t) \cdot N'(t)}}{\parallel \gamma'(t) \parallel} = \frac{{(\gamma'(t) \times \gamma''(t)) \cdot \gamma'''(t)}}{\parallel \gamma'(t) \times \gamma''(t) \parallel^2} \) and is invariant under reparameterization.

The rank of an elliptic curve $E$, denoted by $R_{E}$, is a measure of the number of independent rational points on the curve. The theorem states that the rank is bounded by $R_{E} \leq m + 2a - 1$, where $m$ and $a$ are the counts of primes of multiplicative and additive reduction respectively.

The Nagell-Lutz theorem states that for an elliptic curve defined over the rationals, the torsion points are rational points of finite order. In the example, it implies that the only torsion points on $E_{1}$ are those of order 2.

The conductor $N_{E/{\mathbb{Q}}}$ of an elliptic curve $E$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ is defined as $N_{E/{\mathbb{Q}}} = {\prod\limits_{p}p^{f_{p}}}$, where $f_{p}$ indicates the type of reduction of $E$ at each prime $p$.
For each prime $p$, the reduction type of an elliptic curve $E$ can be classified as good (0), multiplicative (1), or additive (2), with special cases for $p = 2$ and $p = 3$.
The L-series $L(E,s)$ is a complex function associated with an elliptic curve $E$ that encodes number-theoretic information about the curve.
The Tate-Shafarevich group $|Sha|$ measures the failure of the local-to-global principle for rational points on the elliptic curve $E$.
The Tamagawa number $c_p$ is an elementary local factor that counts the number of points on the elliptic curve $E$ over the $p$-adic numbers, reflecting the structure of $E$ at the prime $p$.
The Parity Conjecture states that the root number $w$ of an elliptic curve indicates the parity of its rank, with $w = 1$ if and only if the rank is even.
A cubic curve over a field $K$ is defined by a Weierstrass equation of the form $f(x,y) = y^2 + a_1xy + a_3y - x^3 - a_2x^2 - a_4x - a_6 = 0$.
A singular point $P$ is a node if the slopes of the two tangent lines at $P$ are different, i.e., if $\alpha \neq \beta$.
A singular point $P$ is a cusp if the slopes of the tangent lines at $P$ are the same, i.e., if $\alpha = \beta$.
An elliptic curve $E$ has bad reduction at a prime $p$ if its reduction $\overset{\sim}{E}$ modulo $p$ is singular.
If the reduction $\overset{\sim}{E}$ has a cusp, then $E$ is said to have additive reduction at $p$.
If the reduction $\overset{\sim}{E}$ has a node, then $E$ is said to have multiplicative reduction at $p$.
The reduction is said to be split multiplicative if the slopes of the tangent lines at the node are in $\mathbb{F}_p$.
The reduction is said to be non-split multiplicative if the slopes of the tangent lines at the node are not in $\mathbb{F}_p$.

The criterion states that for an elliptic curve $E/K$ to have good reduction over $K$, several equivalent conditions must hold, including the unramified nature of certain modules and the action of the inertia group.
An elliptic curve $E/K$ has potential good reduction if there exists a finite extension $K'$ of $K$ such that $E/K'$ has good reduction.
The Tate module $T_l(E)$ of an elliptic curve $E$ is a module that captures the $l$-adic properties of the curve, particularly its points of finite order.
An integer factorization algorithm that is effective for numbers with certain properties, particularly those with small prime factors.
A probabilistic algorithm for integer factorization that uses a pseudo-random sequence to find nontrivial factors of a composite number.

Taxicab numbers are defined as the smallest number that can be expressed as a sum of two cubes in $n$ distinct ways. The article references the taxicab number 1729, which is famously associated with a story about mathematician Ramanujan.
Hasse's bound provides a limit on the number of points $N_{q}$ on an elliptic curve over a finite field, stating that ${\mid {N_{q} - q - 1} \mid} \leq {2\sqrt{q}}$.
The L-series of an elliptic curve is a generating function that encodes information about the number of points on the curve over finite fields, with the parameter $a_{p} = {p + 1 - N_{p}}$.
A height function on an abelian group $A$ is a function $h: A \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ satisfying certain properties related to growth and finiteness of sets of points in $A$.
The canonical height of an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ is defined using a limit involving the height function $h_x$ and is crucial for understanding the arithmetic of the curve.
The descent theorem states that if the quotient group $A/mA$ is finite for a height function $h$, then the abelian group $A$ is finitely generated.

A torsion subgroup of an elliptic curve is a subgroup consisting of points that have finite order. According to Mazur's theorem, for elliptic curves defined over the rationals, the possible torsion subgroups are limited to specific forms.

Mazur's theorem states that the only possible torsion subgroups of elliptic curves defined over the rationals are of the forms \( \mathbb{Z}/N\mathbb{Z} \) for \( 1 < N < 11 \) or \( N = 12 \), and \( \mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z} \oplus \mathbb{Z}/2N\mathbb{Z} \) for \( 0 < N < 5 \).
The *invariant differential* is defined as $$\omega = \frac{dx}{2y + a_{1}x + a_{3}} = \frac{dy}{3x^{2} + 2a_{2}x + a_{4} - a_{1}y}$$, which is used in the study of elliptic curves.

A Weierstrass equation for an elliptic curve $E/K$ is a minimal model if the valuation of its discriminant $
u(\Delta)$ equals the minimum value $m$ of the set $S$, where $S$ consists of non-negative integers representing the valuations of discriminants of Weierstrass equations for $E$.
The local part at a prime $p$ of the L-series is defined as $L_{p}(T)$, which varies based on the type of reduction of the elliptic curve at $p$.
The number $w$ in the functional equation of the L-series is called the root number of $E$, which has important conjectural implications, particularly in the Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer conjecture.
A Mordell curve is an elliptic curve $E/K$, for some field $K$, which admits a model by a Weierstrass equation of the form $y^{2} = x^{3} + k$, where $k \in K$.
The Mordell-Weil group of a Mordell curve is the group of $K$-rational points on the elliptic curve, denoted as $E(K)$.
The torsion group of a Mordell curve is the subgroup of points of finite order, which can be isomorphic to groups like $\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$.
A branch of mathematics that studies the representation of functions or signals as the superposition of basic waves.
A theory in probability that provides asymptotic estimates for the probabilities of rare events.
The weak Mordell-Weil theorem is a foundational result that contributes to the proof of the Mordell-Weil theorem, although its specific statement is not provided in the article.

An elliptic curve $E$ over a field of characteristic $p$ defined by the cubic equation ${f{(w,x,y)}} = 0$ is called supersingular if the coefficient of ${({wxy})}^{p - 1}$ in $f{(w,x,y)}^{p - 1}$ is zero.
A supersingular elliptic curve is said to have Hasse invariant $0$; an ordinary elliptic curve is said to have Hasse invariant $1$.
This problem is a foundational aspect of cryptography involving the difficulty of computing logarithms in the group of points of an elliptic curve.

The $\phi$-*Selmer group* is defined as $S^{\phi}({E/{\mathbb{Q}}}) = {Ker}(H^{1}(G,{E{(\overline{\mathbb{Q}})}{\lbrack\phi\rbrack}}) \rightarrow \prod_{p}{H^{1}(G_{p},E)})$, representing elements in $H^{1}(G,{E{(\overline{\mathbb{Q}})}{\lbrack\phi\rbrack}})$ that correspond to local points in $E({\mathbb{Q}}_{p})$.
The $i^{th}$-cohomology group $H^{i}(G,{E{(\overline{\mathbb{Q}})})}$ is used to analyze the structure of the Selmer and Tate-Shafarevich groups.

The reduction of an elliptic curve $E$ modulo a prime $p$, denoted $\overset{\sim}{E}$, is a curve defined over the finite field $\mathbb{F}_{p}$ obtained by reducing the coefficients of the Weierstrass equation modulo $p$.

The set of non-singular points $\overset{\sim}{E}_{ns}(\mathbb{F}_{p})$ of the reduced curve $\overset{\sim}{E}$ consists of points where the curve does not have singularities, which is important for the structure of the group of points.

In topology, the genus \( g(S) \) of an orientable surface \( S \) is defined as the number of 'handles' it has. The sphere has genus 0, and the connected sum of \( n \) tori has genus \( n \). The genus is additive with respect to connected sums.

In algebraic geometry, the genus of a smooth projective curve \( X \) over a field \( k \) is the dimension over \( k \) of the vector space \( \Omega^{1}(X) \) of global regular differentials on \( X \).

The Hurwitz genus formula relates the genera of two smooth curves under a non-constant separable map, providing a condition for the genus of the first curve based on the degree of the map and the ramification indices at points of the first curve.
A non-constant map of curves is separable if the extension of function fields is a separable extension, meaning that it does not introduce inseparable elements.

Concept: space of functions associated to a divisor
Explanation: The space of functions associated to a divisor $D$ on a curve $C/K$ is defined as ${\mathcal{L}(D) = \{f \in \overline{K}(C)^{*} : 	ext{div}(f) \geq -D\} \cup \{0\}}$, where $\overline{K}(C)^{*}$ is the dual to the function field of $C$.

Concept: dimension of the space of functions
Explanation: For any divisor $D$, the space $\mathcal{L}(D)$ is a finite-dimensional vector space over $\overline{K}$, and its dimension is denoted by $\ell(D)$.
For any non-zero number $a$, the expression $a^{0}$ is defined to be equal to 1.
A skyscraper sheaf $\mathcal{E}$ is a type of sheaf that is supported at a single point, capturing local data and allowing for the study of local properties in algebraic geometry.
A projective curve over a field $k$ is defined as an equidimensional projective variety of dimension 1, meaning each irreducible component of the variety has dimension 1.
An *abelian variety* over a field $k$ is a proper group scheme over ${Spec}k$ that is a variety.
The group law on an abelian variety is commutative, meaning that the $R$-points of an abelian variety form an abelian group for every ring $R$.

The *general linear group scheme* ${GL}_{n}$ is defined as the affine scheme given by the quotient of the polynomial ring ${\mathbb{Z}[Y,X_{11},\ldots,X_{nn}]}$ by the ideal generated by the relation $Y \det\begin{pmatrix} X_{11} & \cdots & X_{1n} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ X_{n1} & \cdots & X_{nn} \end{pmatrix} - 1$. An $R$-point of ${GL}_{n}$ corresponds to an invertible matrix with entries in a commutative ring $R$.
Given a ring R, the set of R-points of a group scheme G forms a group.

An elliptic surface over a curve $C/k$ is a geometrical structure that generalizes elliptic curves to higher dimensions, allowing for a family of elliptic curves parameterized by the points of the curve $C$.

The Shioda-Tate formula relates the rank of the Mordell-Weil group of an elliptic surface to the rank of its Néron-Severi group and the number of irreducible components of its fibers, expressed as ${rank}_{\mathbb{Z}}({\mathcal{E}/K}) = {rank}_{\mathbb{Z}}({NS(\mathcal{E})}) - {rank}_{\mathbb{Z}}(T)$.

A group variety $G$ is a variety equipped with morphisms that define a group structure, including a multiplication morphism $\mu$, an inverse morphism $\iota$, and an identity morphism $\epsilon$. It is a group object in the category of varieties.
A right ideal $I$ of a ring $R$ is a nonempty subset such that for all $a, b \in I$, the difference $a - b$ is in $I$, and for all $a \in I$ and $r \in R$, the product $a \cdot r$ is in $I$.
A two-sided ideal is a subset $I$ of a ring $R$ that is both a left ideal and a right ideal.

An affine algebraic group over a field $k$ is a quasi-affine variety $G$ equipped with a group structure such that the multiplication map $m: G 	imes G \rightarrow G$ and the inverse map $i: G \rightarrow G$ are algebraic.
The multiplication map $m: G 	imes G \rightarrow G$ is a function that defines the group operation in an affine algebraic group.
The inverse map $i: G \rightarrow G$ is a function that assigns to each element its inverse in the group structure of an affine algebraic group.
An algebraic torus over $k$ is another example of an affine algebraic group, characterized by its group structure and algebraic properties.

A quiver $Q$ is a directed graph consisting of a set of vertices $Q_{0}$ and a set of arrows $Q_{1}$, along with source and target functions $s$ and $t$ that assign to each arrow its starting and ending vertex.
A quiver morphism $F: Q \rightarrow Q'$ is a pair of functions $(F_{0}, F_{1})$ that map vertices and arrows of one quiver to another while preserving the source and target structure.

A concrete category is a category $\mathcal{C}$ together with a faithful functor $S: \mathcal{C} \rightarrow \mathcal{SET}$ that assigns to each object in $\mathcal{C}$ a set, allowing for the interpretation of morphisms as functions between sets.

A morphism $F: Q \rightarrow Q'$ is injective in the context of the concrete category $(\mathcal{Q}, S)$ if both component functions $F_{0}$ and $F_{1}$ are injective. The same applies for surjectivity.

A morphism $F: Q \rightarrow Q^{\prime}$ is an isomorphism if there exists a morphism $G: Q^{\prime} \rightarrow Q$ such that $FG = Id(Q^{\prime})$ and $GF = Id(Q)$, meaning $F$ and $G$ are inverses of each other.

The identity morphism $Id(Q)$ for a quiver $Q$ is defined as $Id(Q) = (Id(Q_{0}), Id(Q_{1}))$, where $Id(Q_{0})$ and $Id(Q_{1})$ are the identity functions on the sets of vertices and arrows, respectively.
A morphism of quivers $F: Q \rightarrow Q'$ is a structure-preserving map that associates vertices and arrows of one quiver to another, maintaining the source and target relationships.

The First Isomorphism Theorem for quivers states that the mapping $\overline{F}: (Q/\sim) \rightarrow Im(F)$ is an isomorphism of quivers, where $Q/\sim$ is the quotient quiver formed by the equivalence relation $\sim$.
The image of a quiver $Im(F)$ under a morphism $F$ consists of the vertices and arrows that are mapped from the original quiver $Q$ to the target quiver $Q'$.

A quiver $Q = (Q_{0}, Q_{1}, s, t)$ is said to be locally finite if for any vertex $a \in Q_{0}$, there is a finite number of neighbours of $a$, which means there are finite numbers of arrows ending in $a$ and starting from $a$.
A finite quiver is a quiver that has a finite number of vertices and arrows, and it is always locally finite.
The path algebra $kQ$ associated with a quiver $Q$ over a field $k$ is the vector space spanned by paths in the quiver, with multiplication defined by concatenation of paths.
The arrow ideal $R_{Q}$ in the path algebra $kQ$ is the two-sided ideal generated by all paths of length 1, which corresponds to all arrows in the quiver.
An ideal $I$ in $kQ$ is admissible if there exists an integer $m \geqslant 2$ such that $R_{Q}^{m} \subseteq I \subseteq R_{Q}^{2}$, allowing for the identification of certain paths as equivalent.
A representation of a quiver $(Q,I)$ is a way of associating a vector space to each vertex and a linear map to each arrow, subject to certain relations defined by the ideal $I$.
An acyclic quiver is a quiver that does not contain any directed cycles, which ensures certain properties about the representations and modules associated with it.
The Kronecker delta $\delta_{bc}$ is a function of two variables that is 1 if they are equal and 0 otherwise, often used in summations and evaluations.
A disconnected quiver is a quiver that can be expressed as a disjoint union of two quivers, meaning there are no paths connecting vertices from one quiver to the other.

An isomorphism of algebras is a bijective linear map that preserves the algebraic operations (addition and multiplication) between two algebras, in this case between $kQ$ and the product $kQ^{\prime} 	imes kQ^{\operatorname{\prime\prime}}$.
A path in a quiver is a sequence of arrows $(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n)$ such that the target of each arrow matches the source of the next arrow in the sequence.
Two paths $w$ and $u$ in a quiver are said to be compatible if the target of the last arrow of $w$ is the same as the source of the first arrow of $u$.
The linear map $\overline{F}: kQ \rightarrow kQ'$ is defined on a basis of the path algebra $kQ$ by mapping paths according to the morphism $F$.

A linear map $\overline{F}$ is a homomorphism of algebras if it preserves the multiplication of paths, meaning $\overline{F}(w \cdot u) = \overline{F}(w) \cdot \overline{F}(u)$ for compatible paths $w$ and $u$.
A bound quiver is a quiver $(Q,I)$ over a base field $k$ where $I$ is an ideal in the path algebra $kQ$.
A morphism $F:(Q,I) \rightarrow (Q',I')$ is a map such that the induced linear map $\overline{F}: kQ \rightarrow kQ'$ satisfies $\overline{F}(I) \subseteq I'$.

If there exists a path from vertex $a$ to vertex $b$, then $a$ is a predecessor of $b$, and $b$ is a successor of $a$. A direct predecessor/successor relationship exists if there is a path of length 1 (an arrow) between them.
A vertex $a$ is a direct predecessor of vertex $b$ if there is an arrow from $a$ to $b$, making $b$ a direct successor of $a$.
The set of neighbours of a vertex $a$ is defined as $a^{-} \cup a^{+}$, where $a^{-}$ is the set of direct predecessors and $a^{+}$ is the set of direct successors of $a$.
An algebra is finite dimensional if it has a finite basis as a vector space over its field, which is the case for ${kQ}/I$ when $Q$ is finite.
A sequence of modules and homomorphisms $0 	o A 	o B 	o C 	o 0$ is exact if the image of each homomorphism equals the kernel of the next, indicating a relationship between the modules.
In the context of a quiver $Q$, a stationary path $e_{x}$ at vertex $x \in Q_{0}$ represents an identity morphism that corresponds to the vertex itself.

A morphism from a quiver $Q$ to a quiver $Q'$ is a pair $F = (F_{0}, F_{1})$ of functions such that $F_{0}: Q_{0} \rightarrow Q_{0}'$ and $F_{1}: Q_{1} \rightarrow Q_{1}'$ satisfy the conditions $s'(F_{1}(\alpha)) = F_{0}(s(\alpha))$ and $t'(F_{1}(\alpha)) = F_{0}(t(\alpha))$.

If $F: Q \rightarrow Q'$ and $G: Q' \rightarrow Q''$ are morphisms between quivers, their composition $G \circ F: Q \rightarrow Q''$ is defined by $(G \circ F) = (G_{0} \circ F_{0}, G_{1} \circ F_{1})$.
A relation in a quiver $Q$ is a linear combination of paths of length at least 2 with the same source and target, represented as $\rho = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_{i} \cdot w_{i}$.
A zero relation is a relation of the form $\rho = w$ for some path $w$ in the quiver.

A representation $\mathbb{V}$ of a quiver $Q$ over a field $k$ is a family of vector spaces ${\{ V_{i}\}}_{i \in Q_{0}}$ along with $k$-linear maps ${\{ f_{a}:\left. V_{s{(a)}}\rightarrow V_{t{(a)}} \right.\}}_{a \in Q_{1}}$.

A morphism $F:\left. {\mathbb{V}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{W}} \right.$ between representations is a family of $k$-linear maps ${\{ F_{i}:\left. V_{i}\rightarrow W_{i} \right.\}}_{i \in Q_{0}}$ satisfying the relation ${{F_{t{(a)}} \circ g_{a}} = {h_{a} \circ F_{s{(a)}}}}$ for each arrow $a \in Q_{1}$.
A representation $\mathbb{V}$ of $Q$ is called trivial if $V_{i} = 0$ for each vertex $i \in Q_{0}$.
A representation $\mathbb{V}$ of $Q$ is locally finite-dimensional if ${\dim_{k}V_{i}} < \infty$ for each vertex $i \in Q_{0}$.
A representation $\mathbb{V}$ of $Q$ is finite-dimensional if it is locally finite-dimensional and $V_{i} = 0$ for almost all vertices $i \in Q_{0}$.

The quotient quiver $Q/ \sim$ is formed by taking the equivalence relation $\sim$ on $Q$ and creating a new quiver $(Q_{0}/ \sim_{0}, Q_{1}/ \sim_{1}, s', t')$ where $s'$ and $t'$ are the induced source and target functions on the equivalence classes.
The quotient morphism $\pi: Q \rightarrow Q/ \sim$ is a morphism of quivers defined by the quotient maps $\pi_{0}$ and $\pi_{1}$ that relate the original quiver $Q$ to its quotient quiver $Q/ \sim$.

A subquiver $Q^{\prime} = {(Q_{0}^{\prime},Q_{1}^{\prime},s^{\prime},t^{\prime})}$ of a quiver $Q$ is defined by subsets $Q_{0}^{\prime} \subseteq Q_{0}$ and $Q_{1}^{\prime} \subseteq Q_{1}$, with the condition that the source and target of each arrow in $Q_{1}^{\prime}$ must also be in $Q_{0}^{\prime}$.
A subquiver $Q^{\prime}$ is called full if it contains all arrows between any two vertices in $Q_{0}^{\prime}$ that exist in $Q_{1}$, meaning it inherits all arrows between points in the subset.
The mapping $i = {(i_{0},i_{1})}$, where both $i_{0}$ and $i_{1}$ are inclusions, is called the inclusion morphism when $Q^{\prime}$ is a subquiver of $Q$.

For a morphism of quivers $F: Q \rightarrow Q^{\prime}$, the image $Im(F)$ is defined as $({Im(F_{0}), Im(F_{1}), s^{\operatorname{\prime\prime}}, t^{\operatorname{\prime\prime}})}$, where $s^{\operatorname{\prime\prime}}$ and $t^{\operatorname{\prime\prime}}$ are the restrictions of $s^{\prime}$ and $t^{\prime}$ to $Im(F_{1})$, and it is a subquiver of $Q^{\prime}$.
For stationary paths, the evaluation map is defined as $f_{e_{x}}: V_{x} \rightarrow V_{x}$ by $f_{e_{x}} = 0$.
The map $f_{\rho}: V_{s(w_{i})} \rightarrow V_{t(w_{i})}$ is a linear map associated with the relation $\rho$.
The ideal $I$ is generated by relations $\{\rho_{1},\ldots,\rho_{n}\}$ in the context of a bound quiver.
A representation $\mathbb{V}$ of $Q$ is said to be bound by $I$ if $f_{\rho_{i}} = 0$ for every relation $\rho_{i}$ in $I$.

A pair $(Q,\tau)$ is a translation quiver if $\tau: X \rightarrow Y$ is a bijection and the number of arrows from a direct predecessor $y$ of $x \in X$ to $x$ equals the number of arrows from $\tau(x)$ to $y$.
A pair $(Q',\tau')$ is a translation subquiver if it is a translation quiver, $Q'$ is a full subquiver of $Q$, and $\tau'(x) = \tau(x)$ for vertices in $Q'$ where $\tau(x)$ exists.
A local complex analytic variety $V$ is normal at a point $p$ if every weakly holomorphic function through $V$ extends to be holomorphic in a neighborhood of $p$.
A function that is holomorphic on a dense subset of its domain but may not be holomorphic everywhere in the neighborhood.
A function that is complex differentiable in a neighborhood of every point in its domain.
The total quotient ring $\mathcal{M}_{p}$ of $\mathcal{O}_{p}$ consists of the germs of meromorphic functions at the point $p$.

The underlying graph $G$ of a quiver $Q$ is defined as $G = (V, E, 	au)$ where $V = Q_{0}$, $E = Q_{1}$, and $	au: E \rightarrow V_{sym}^{2}$ is a function that maps each arrow to its source and target, forgetting the orientation of arrows.
The path algebra $kQ$ is unitary if it has an identity element, which occurs if and only if the quiver $Q$ has a finite number of vertices.

The Borel-Bott-Weil theorem states that if $({\lambda + \rho},\alpha) = 0$ for any simple root $\alpha$ of $\mathfrak{g}$, then $H^{i}(\mathcal{L}_{\lambda}) = 0$ for all $i$. Otherwise, if $w \in W$ is such that $w({\lambda + \rho})$ is dominant, then $H^{\ell(w)}(\mathcal{L}_{\lambda}) \cong V_{\lambda}$, where $V_{\lambda}$ is the unique irreducible representation of highest weight $\lambda$, and $H^{i}(\mathcal{L}_{\lambda}) = 0$ for all other $i$.
A Lie group $G$ is a group that is also a differentiable manifold, allowing for the use of calculus in the study of its structure and representations.
The Weyl group $W$ of a Lie group $G$ is a group generated by reflections corresponding to the roots of the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$, playing a crucial role in the representation theory of $G$.

The sheaf cohomology groups $H^{i}(\mathcal{L}_{\lambda})$ measure the global sections of the line bundle $\mathcal{L}_{\lambda}$ and their relationships, providing insight into the geometric and topological properties of the bundle.

An integral weight $\lambda$ for a semisimple Lie group $G$ is a weight that can be expressed as a linear combination of the simple roots with integer coefficients, used to define representations of $G$.
A weight $\lambda$ is dominant if it satisfies the condition $({\lambda + \rho},\alpha) > 0$ for all simple roots $\alpha$, indicating that it corresponds to a certain class of representations.
The (partial) flag variety \( \mathcal{F}\ell{(V,\mathbf{i})} \) is the set of all flags \( \{ 0 \} \leq V_{1} \subset \cdots \subset V_{n} \) with \( \dim V_{j} = i_{j} \).
The flag variety has a natural embedding into the product of Grassmannians \( G{(V,i_{1})} \times \cdots \times G{(V,i_{m})} \).
The stabilizer of a point in the action of \( Sl{(V)} \) on the flag variety is a parabolic subgroup, denoted as \( P \).
The complete flag variety is isomorphic to \( Sl{(V)}/B \), where \( B \) is the Borel subgroup of \( Sl{(V)} \).
A variety $X$ is normal at a point $p \in X$ if the local ring $\mathcal{O}_{p}$ is integrally closed. A variety is normal if it is normal at every point.

The Cartan's umbrella is a class of real analytic sets in ${\mathbb{R}}^{3}$ that are irreducible and contain regular points of both dimension 1 and dimension 2. A common equation for a Cartan umbrella is ${{z({x^{2} + y^{2}}) - y^{3}} = 0}$, which can be solved for $z$ as $z = \frac{y^{3}}{x^{2} + y^{2}}$. The umbrella includes the $z$ axis, as all points of the form $(0,0,z)$ satisfy the equation.
A mathematical problem posed by David Hilbert that concerns the foundations of mathematics and the existence of certain types of solutions to differential equations.
A set of nonlinear partial differential equations that describe the motion of fluid substances such as liquids and gases.
Hodge theory is a branch of algebraic geometry and complex manifold theory that deals with the decomposition of the cohomology groups of a complex projective algebraic variety.
A form is called harmonic if its Laplacian is zero, which relates to the search for differential forms of minimal length.
The Laplacian operator, denoted as $\Delta$, is defined as $\Delta = d\phi + \phi d$, where $\phi$ is the adjoint operator to $d$ with respect to the Riemannian metric.

The Hodge decomposition states that the space of harmonic $k$-forms can be decomposed into components of $(p,q)$-forms, leading to a decomposition of the space of harmonic forms as $H_{\Delta}^{k} = \bigoplus_{p + q = k} H_{\Delta}^{p,q}$.

Kahler Manifolds are complex manifolds equipped with a Riemannian metric that is compatible with the complex structure, and they are a class of manifolds where the cross terms in the Laplacian expression vanish.

A projective line configuration consists of a collection of $p$ points and $\ell$ lines in a projective space such that through each point there pass $\lambda$ lines and on each line there are $\pi$ points.

A self-dual configuration is one where the dual configuration is projectively equivalent to the original configuration, meaning that the roles of points and lines can be interchanged while preserving incidence.

An abstract line configuration is defined as a triplet $\langle P,L,I\rangle$, where $P$ and $L$ are sets of points and lines, respectively, and $I$ is a relation indicating incidence between points and lines.
The dual configuration of an abstract line configuration $\langle P,L,I\rangle$ is $\langle L,P,I^{\prime}\rangle$, where $I^{\prime}(x,y)$ holds if and only if $I(y,x)$ holds.
A symmetry of a configuration is a collineation that maps the configuration onto itself, preserving both points and lines.
A counting argument is used to derive the relation $p \cdot \lambda = \pi \cdot \ell$, counting pairs of points and lines in two different ways to establish equality.
An inflection point on a curve is a point where the tangent intersects the curve with multiplicity 3, which occurs if and only if it is a zero of the Hessian.
A non-singular cubic curve is a cubic curve that does not have any singular points, meaning it has a tangent at all points.
The Hessian of a function is a determinant that provides information about the curvature of the function at a point; it is used to determine the nature of critical points.
Canonical forms are standard representations of mathematical objects that simplify calculations and reveal structural properties; for cubic curves, the Weierstrass form is a common canonical form.
The Weierstrass canonical form is a specific representation of a non-singular cubic curve that simplifies the analysis of its properties, including inflection points.
Cubic curves are algebraic curves defined by polynomial equations of degree three, which can exhibit complex geometric properties, including inflection points.
A singularity in a curve is a point where the curve fails to be smooth, meaning it does not have a well-defined tangent.

A semialgebraic set is a subset $A \subset {\mathbb{R}}^{n}$ defined by real polynomials $p_{j\ell}$ and relations $\epsilon_{j\ell}$, where $\epsilon_{j\ell}$ can be $>$, $=$, or $<$. It can be expressed as a finite union of sets defined by these polynomials and inequalities.

The Tarski-Seidenberg theorem states that semialgebraic sets are closed under projection, meaning that the projection of a semialgebraic set onto a lower-dimensional space is also a semialgebraic set.
On a dense open subset of a semialgebraic set $A$, it behaves like a submanifold, allowing for the definition of its dimension as the largest dimension at points where $A$ is a submanifold.

A closed set $X \subset U$ is called a real analytic subvariety of $U$ if for each point $p \in X$, there exists a neighbourhood $V$ and a set $\mathcal{F}$ of real analytic functions defined in $V$, such that $X \cap V = \{p \in V \mid f(p) = 0 \text{ for all } f \in \mathcal{F}\}$.
If $U = \mathbb{R}^{N}$ and all the $f \in \mathcal{F}$ are real polynomials, then $X$ is said to be a real algebraic subvariety.
If $X$ is not required to be closed, then it is said to be a local real analytic subvariety.
A point $p \in X$ is called a regular point if there is a neighbourhood $V$ of $p$ such that $X \cap V$ is a submanifold.
The set of regular points of $X$ is denoted by $X^{-}$ or sometimes $X^{*}$.
The set of singular points is no longer a subvariety as in the complex case, though it can be shown to be semianalytic.

An algebraic manifold $M$ is a submanifold of $k^n$ that corresponds to an irreducible algebraic variety $V$ with the same dimension, such that $M$ is contained within $V$. If the field $k$ is the real numbers $\mathbb{R}$, then $M$ is referred to as a Nash manifold.
A Nash manifold is a specific type of algebraic manifold where the field $k$ is the real numbers $\mathbb{R}$, indicating that it possesses properties related to real algebraic geometry.
Semialgebraic sets are subsets of \( \mathbb{R}^n \) defined by a finite number of polynomial equalities and inequalities.
Łojasiewicz generalized the Tarski-Seidenberg theorem by extending the closure properties of semialgebraic sets to certain sets defined in terms of real-valued functions.
A ring of real valued functions on a set \( U \) is a collection of functions that includes addition and multiplication, and is closed under these operations.

The smallest set of subsets \( \mathcal{S}(\mathcal{A}(U)) \) contains sets defined by inequalities \( \{x \in U \mid f(x) > 0\} \) for all \( f \in \mathcal{A}(U) \) and is closed under finite unions, intersections, and complements.
A smooth submanifold is a subset of a manifold that is itself a manifold and has continuous derivatives of all orders, denoted as $C^{\infty}$.
The theorem states that if $M$ is a connected smooth submanifold contained in a real analytic subvariety $V$ of the same dimension, then $M$ is also a real analytic submanifold.

The Jacobian determinant, denoted as $\det{({{\partial f_{i}}/{\partial x_{j}}})}$, is a polynomial that represents the determinant of the matrix of first-order partial derivatives of a polynomial map. It is crucial in determining the invertibility of the map.
The Jacobian conjecture posits that if a polynomial map $F: {\mathbb{C}}^{n} \rightarrow {\mathbb{C}}^{n}$ has a Jacobian determinant that is a non-zero constant, then the map is invertible.

Affine space (or affine $n$-space) is defined as the set ${\{{(k_{1},\ldots,k_{n})}:{k_{i} \in K}\}}$, where $K$ is a field and $n$ is a positive integer. It is denoted by $K^{n}$ or $A^{n}$, and is considered a topological space with the Zariski topology.
An anti-diagonal matrix is defined as a square matrix where all entries are zero except for those on the anti-diagonal, which runs from the lower left corner to the upper right corner.
An entry in a square matrix that lies on the anti-diagonal line, which extends from the lower left corner to the upper right corner of the matrix.

If $A$ and $D$ are $n \times n$ anti-diagonal and diagonal matrices respectively, then the products ${AD}$ and ${DA}$ are also anti-diagonal. Additionally, the product of two anti-diagonal matrices results in a diagonal matrix.

The $r^{th}$ *compound matrix* of an $m 	imes n$ matrix $A$ is defined as the $\binom{m}{r} 	imes \binom{n}{r}$ matrix whose entries are the determinants of submatrices of $A$, denoted as $\det A[\alpha,\beta]$, where $\alpha \in Q_{r,m}$ and $\beta \in Q_{r,n}$, arranged in lexicographic order. It is denoted by $C_{r}(A)$.

Several properties of compound matrices include: (1) $C_{r}(AB) = C_{r}(A)C_{r}(B)$ for compatible matrices $A$ and $B$; (2) If $A$ is nonsingular, then $C_{r}(A)^{-1} = C_{r}(A^{-1})$; (3) For complex matrices, $C_{r}(A^{*}) = (C_{r}(A))^{*}$; (4) $C_{r}(A^{T}) = (C_{r}(A))^{T}$; (5) $C_{r}(\overline{A}) = \overline{C_{r}(A)}$; (6) $C_{r}(kA) = k^{r}C_{r}(A)$ for any scalar $k$; (7) $C_{r}(I_{n}) = I_{(\binom{n}{r})}$; (8) The determinant of the compound matrix satisfies $\det(C_{r}(A)) = \det(A)^{(\binom{n - 1}{r - 1})}$, known as the Sylvester - Franke theorem.
An orthogonal matrix $A$ satisfies the condition $A^{-1} = A^{T}$, meaning its inverse is equal to its transpose.
Schur decomposition expresses a matrix A as A = PTP^t, where P is an orthogonal matrix and T is an upper triangular matrix.
Eigenvectors are non-zero vectors X such that (A - λI)X = 0 for each eigenvalue λ.
Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization is a method for converting a set of linearly independent vectors into an orthonormal set.
An orthonormal set consists of vectors that are both orthogonal to each other and of unit length.

The Legendre Transform of a function $f$ is defined as $\mathcal{L}f = g: \mathbb{R}^{n} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$, where $g(y) = \varphi(y) \cdot y - f(\varphi(y))$, allowing the transition from a function of variables $x$ to a function of the derivatives of $f$.
The Inverse Mapping Theorem is used to ensure that the transformation can be inverted, which is crucial for defining the Legendre Transform.
The Helmholtz Potential is defined as $F = U - TS$, where $U$ is the internal energy, $T$ is temperature, and $S$ is entropy, allowing the substitution of entropy with temperature.
Enthalpy is defined as $H = U + PV$, where $P$ is pressure and $V$ is volume, allowing the substitution of volume with pressure.

The Hamiltonian is defined as $H = L - p \cdot v$, where $L$ is the Lagrangian, $p$ is the generalized momentum, and $v$ is the generalized velocity, representing the energy of the system in terms of generalized coordinates and momenta.
Matrices $A$ and $B$ are said to be conformally partitioned for multiplication if their submatrices are arranged such that the dimensions align for matrix multiplication.

The method of computing the product of conformally partitioned matrices $A$ and $B$ is referred to as block multiplication, where the resulting matrix $C$ is partitioned into submatrices $C^{i,j}$ defined by $C^{i,j} = \sum_{t=1}^{k} A^{i,t} B^{t,j}$.
For matrices $A$ and $B$, the dimensions are defined such that $A \in M_{m,n}(R)$ and $B \in M_{n,p}(R)$, where the dimensions of the submatrices are specified as $A^{i,j}$ and $B^{i,j}$.
A covector is a linear map \( \alpha: V \rightarrow k \) from a vector space \( V \) over a field \( k \) to the field itself, representing an element of the dual space to \( V \).
A matrix $A \in \mathbb{C}^{n \times n}$ is a normal matrix if it commutes with its conjugate transpose, i.e., $AA^{H} = A^{H}A$.
A unitary matrix $Q \in \mathbb{C}^{n \times n}$ satisfies $Q^{H}Q = I$, where $I$ is the identity matrix, and $Q^{H}$ is the conjugate transpose of $Q$.
A diagonal matrix is a matrix in which the entries outside the main diagonal are all zero, represented as $diag(\lambda_{1}, \ldots, \lambda_{n})$.

If $A$ is a complex matrix, then the *conjugate transpose* $A^{\ast}$ is the matrix $A^{\ast} = {\overline{A}}^{	ext{T}}$, where $\overline{A}$ is the complex conjugate of $A$, and $A^{	ext{T}}$ is the transpose of $A$. For real matrices, the conjugate transpose coincides with the transpose.

Several properties include: 1) ${({{\alpha A} + {\beta B}})}^{\ast} = {{\overline{\alpha}A^{\ast}} + {\overline{\beta}B^{\ast}}}$, $A^{\ast \ast} = A$; 2) ${{({AB})}^{\ast} = {B^{\ast}A^{\ast}}$; 3) For a complex square matrix $A$, $\det{(A^{\ast})} = \overline{\det A}$, ${trace}{(A^{\ast})} = \overline{{trace}⁡A}$, ${(A^{\ast})}^{- 1} = {(A^{- 1})}^{\ast}$; 4) $\langle{Ax},y\rangle = \langle x,{A^{\ast}y}\rangle$.
A reflection matrix is a transformation matrix that reflects points across a specified line in a plane. The matrix can be derived based on the angle of the line or its direction vector.
Orthogonal matrices are square matrices whose rows and columns are orthogonal unit vectors. They preserve lengths and angles, and the determinant of a reflection matrix is -1.

The real projective line, denoted as \( \mathbb{R}\mathbb{P}^{1} \), is formed by taking the circle and identifying opposite points, representing unique mirror lines of reflection in \( \mathbb{R}^{2} \).
Change of coordinates refers to the process of transforming the representation of vectors and matrices from one coordinate system to another, often using transformation matrices.
The matrix product is the result of multiplying two matrices together, which can be used to derive transformation matrices such as those for reflections.
The digits of $\pi$ are infinite in any base, and any finite group of digits can be found as a substring in the digits of $\pi$ if one searches sufficiently far.
The Pi-Search Page allows users to search for specific digit substrings within the first 200 million base 10 digits of $\pi$, returning the desired substring along with surrounding digits.
This sequence lists the first occurrence of $n$'s base 10 digits as a substring in $\pi$, highlighting the relationship between integers and the digits of $\pi$.
Graeme McRae investigated overlaps in the first hundred thousand digits of $\pi$, finding that certain substrings, like '112', contain overlapping sequences, but only a few such overlaps exist.

The degree of homogeneity $r$ indicates how the function scales when its input is scaled by a factor $\lambda$; specifically, it describes the power to which $\lambda$ is raised in the homogeneity condition.

The rotation matrix for a two-dimensional vector is given by the formula: $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$, which rotates a vector by an angle $\phi$ counter-clockwise.

The angle addition formula states that $\cos(\theta + \phi) = \cos\theta \cos\phi - \sin\theta \sin\phi$ and $\sin(\theta + \phi) = \sin\theta \cos\phi + \cos\theta \sin\phi$, which is used to derive the rotated vector.

The sign of a permutation $sgn(n, n - 1, \ldots, 1)$ is calculated based on the number of transpositions needed to arrange the elements in the natural order, affecting the overall sign of the determinant.
The matrix exponential of a diagonal matrix is computed as $e^{diag(a_{1},\ldots,a_{n})} = diag(e^{a_{1}},\ldots,e^{a_{n}})$.
If $A$ is a diagonal matrix, then its adjugate is also a diagonal matrix.
The eigenspace $E_{\lambda}$ associated with an eigenvalue $\lambda$ is defined as $E_{\lambda} = \{u \in V : Tu = \lambda u\}$, and it is a subspace of $V$.

A transformation $T$ is nilpotent if there exists some integer $k$ such that $T^k = 0$. In the context of diagonalization, it can contribute to the failure of $T$ being diagonalizable if it acts on an invariant subspace.
An $n \times n$ matrix $M = {(m_{ij})}$ is an *equitable matrix* if all $m_{ij}$ are positive, and $m_{ij} = {m_{ik}m_{kj}}$ for all ${{i,j,k} = 1},{\ldots,n}$.
The diagonal elements of equitable matrices equal $1$ as shown by setting $i = j = k$ in the defining equation.

If $M$ is an $n \times n$ equitable matrix and $s:\left. {\{ 1,\ldots,r\}}\rightarrow{\{ 1,\ldots,n\}} \right.$ is a mapping, then $K_{ab} = {M_{s{(a)}s{(b)}},a},{b = {1,\ldots,r}}$ is an equitable $r \times r$ matrix.

For a matrix $M = \begin{pmatrix} m_{11} & m_{12} \\ m_{21} & m_{22} \end{pmatrix}$ to be equitable, it must satisfy the conditions $m_{11}, m_{12}, m_{21}, m_{22} > 0$ and a set of equations relating its elements.

A square matrix $A$ of order $n$ is *diagonally dominant* if for each row $i$, the absolute value of the diagonal entry $|a_{ii}|$ is greater than or equal to the sum of the absolute values of the other entries in that row: $|a_{ii}| \geq \sum_{j=1, j 
eq i}^{n} |a_{ij}|$.

A square matrix $A$ of order $n$ is *strictly diagonally dominant* if for each row $i$, the absolute value of the diagonal entry $|a_{ii}|$ is greater than the sum of the absolute values of the other entries in that row: $|a_{ii}| > \sum_{j=1, j 
eq i}^{n} |a_{ij}|$.
The linear least squares method assumes a linear relationship between measurements $z$ and estimated variables $x$, represented by the equation $z = Mx + b$.
The fitting equation derived from the linear least squares solution is given by $x_{fit} = (A^{T}A)^{-1}A^{T}z$, which minimizes the mean square of the error.
Matrix $A$ is formed as a combination of an identity vector and the column vector $x$, expressed as $A = [1|x]$.

A matrix is diagonalizable if it can be expressed in the form $A = UDU^{-1}$, where $D$ is a diagonal matrix and $U$ is an invertible matrix. Some matrices may not be diagonalizable over the reals but can be over the complex numbers.
Braid groups are algebraic structures that capture the concept of braiding strands. Ruth Lawrence is known for proving that these groups are linear, meaning they can be represented as matrices.
Combinatorial knot theory studies knots and links using combinatorial techniques, focusing on their properties and classifications.
Khovanov theory is a categorification of the Jones polynomial, providing a deeper understanding of knot invariants through homological algebra.
Discrete mathematics deals with countable, distinct, and separable objects, often involving structures such as graphs and integers.
The slope of a line is a measure of its steepness and is defined as the ratio of the rise over the run. For a line with slope $m$, the slope of a line perpendicular to it is given by $\frac{-1}{m}$.
An *exactly determined* system of linear equations has precisely as many unknowns as equations, making it soluble.
The Hadamard conjecture posits that a Hadamard matrix of order $n$ exists for all $n = 4m$, where $m$ is a positive integer.
Paley's theorem states that there exists a Hadamard matrix $H_{n}$ when $n$ is divisible by 4 and of the form $2^{e}({p^{m} + 1})$, with $p$ being an odd prime and $e, m$ being positive integers.
The determinant of a Hadamard matrix satisfies the inequality $det{(X)} \leq n^{n/2}$ for a matrix $X = {(x_{ij})}$ of order $n$ with $|x_{ij}| \leq 1$ for all $i$ and $j$.

1. The order of a Hadamard matrix is $1, 2$, or $4n$, where $n$ is an integer. 2. Permuting the rows and columns retains the Hadamard property. 3. Multiplying any row or column by $-1$ retains the Hadamard property.
A Hadamard matrix is said to be normalized if its first row and first column contain only $+1$ entries.
The graph of the equation $xy = c$ is a hyperbola, characterized by having two asymptotes and being of second degree as indicated by the equation $xy - c = 0$.
Lines that the graph of the hyperbola approaches but never touches, which are crucial in understanding the behavior of the hyperbola as $x$ or $y$ approaches infinity.
The transformation of the hyperbola into a canonical form through a rotation of the coordinate axes, resulting in the equation $\frac{x^{\prime 2}}{2c} - \frac{y^{\prime 2}}{2c} = 1$.
A specific type of hyperbola represented by the equation $\frac{x^{\prime 2}}{2c} - \frac{y^{\prime 2}}{2c} = 1$, where the transverse and conjugate axes are aligned with the coordinate axes.

The *free vector space over a set* $X$, denoted $C(X)$, consists of functions $f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{K}$ that are non-zero at finitely many points in $X$. It serves as a vector space generated by the set $X$.

The functions $\Delta_a$ defined for each $a \in X$ form a linearly independent basis for $C(X)$, where $C(X) = \operatorname{span}\{\Delta_a\}_{a \in X}$ consists of all finite linear combinations of these basis elements.

The mapping $\iota: X \rightarrow C(X)$ is universal, meaning for any mapping $\phi$ from $X$ to a vector space $V$, there exists a unique linear mapping $\overline{\phi}$ such that the diagram commutes.
A supertriangular matrix is a square matrix where all entries below the main diagonal are zero.
The zero vector $0_{V}$ in a vector space $V$ is the unique vector such that adding it to any vector $v$ yields $v$ itself.

A subspace $U \subset V$ is called a $T$-invariant subspace if ${T{(u)}} \in U$ for all $u \in U$, meaning that applying the linear transformation $T$ to any vector in $U$ results in a vector that is also in $U$.
Any skew-symmetric matrix of odd order has determinant equal to $0$.
A matrix $A$ is skew-symmetric if $A^{T} = -A$. For odd-order matrices, this implies that their determinant is zero.
An antisymmetric matrix is another term for a skew-symmetric matrix, where $A = -A^{T}$, and for $n=3$, can be represented in a specific form involving a vector.

The Kronecker product of two matrices $A$ and $B$ is defined as the block matrix $A \otimes B = \begin{pmatrix} a_{11}B & \cdots & a_{1n}B \ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \ a_{n1}B & \cdots & a_{nn}B \end{pmatrix}$, where $A$ is an $n 	imes n$ matrix and $B$ is an $m 	imes m$ matrix.
The Kronecker product is bilinear, meaning $A \otimes (B + C) = A \otimes B + A \otimes C$ and $(B + C) \otimes A = B \otimes A + C \otimes A$ for square matrices $A$, $B$, and $C$.

Concept: Existence of products involving Kronecker products
Explanation: If $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$ are square matrices such that the products $AC$ and $BD$ exist, then $(A \otimes B)(C \otimes D) = (AC \otimes BD)$.
If $A$ and $B$ are invertible matrices, then the inverse of their Kronecker product is given by $(A \otimes B)^{-1} = A^{-1} \otimes B^{-1}$.
For square matrices $A$ and $B$, the transpose of their Kronecker product satisfies $(A \otimes B)^{T} = A^{T} \otimes B^{T}$.

If $A$ and $B$ are square matrices with eigenvalues $\{\lambda_{i}\}$ and $\{\mu_{j}\}$ respectively, then the eigenvalues of $A \otimes B$ are given by $\{\lambda_{i} \mu_{j}\}$ for $i = 1,\ldots,n$ and $j = 1,\ldots,m$.
The determinant of the Kronecker product is given by $\det(A \otimes B) = (\det A)^{m} (\det B)^{n}$, where $A$ is $n 	imes n$ and $B$ is $m 	imes m$.
The rank of the Kronecker product satisfies $\operatorname{rank}(A \otimes B) = \operatorname{rank}(A) \cdot \operatorname{rank}(B)$.
The trace of the Kronecker product is given by $\operatorname{trace}(A \otimes B) = \operatorname{trace}(A) \cdot \operatorname{trace}(B)$.

The Levy-Desplanques theorem states that a strictly diagonally dominant matrix is non-singular, meaning that if a matrix $A$ satisfies the condition $|a_{ii}| > \sum_{j \neq i}|a_{ij}|$ for all $i$, then its determinant $\det(A) \neq 0$.

The Gerschgorin circle theorem provides a method for locating the eigenvalues of a matrix by associating them with circles in the complex plane, where each circle is centered at the diagonal entry $a_{ii}$ with a radius equal to the sum of the absolute values of the off-diagonal entries in the same row.

The least squares method aims to find a set of parameters $x$ that minimizes the sum of squares of the differences between measured values $y$ and the values predicted by a set of equations $f(x,y)$. This is expressed mathematically as minimizing $f(x,y) = 0$ with respect to $\Delta y$.
When the measurements $y$ are correlated, the variances and correlations are represented by a covariance matrix $C$. The least squares condition then becomes minimizing $\Delta y^{T}C^{-1}\Delta y$.
If there is one equation for each measurement and the functions $f$ are linear in the elements of $y$ and $x$, the solution is referred to as linear regression, a specific case of least squares.
A linear automorphism $J$ on a real vector space $V$ is a linear transformation that is invertible and satisfies $J^{2} = -{id}_{V}$.
A complex structure $J$ allows the real vector space $V$ to be treated as a complex vector space, enabling operations involving complex numbers.
The complex structure $J_{0}$ can be represented by the matrix $$\begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I}_{n} \\ {- \mathbf{I}_{n}} & \mathbf{0} \end{pmatrix}$$ in the case of $V = \mathbb{R}^{2n}$.
Two matrices $A$ and $B$ are said to commute if $AB = BA$. This property is essential for the behavior of their inverses.

A matrix $B$ is invertible if there exists a matrix $B^{-1}$ such that $BB^{-1} = I$, where $I$ is the identity matrix. The theorem states that if $B$ is invertible and $A$ commutes with $B$, then $A$ also commutes with $B^{-1}$.
Linear algebra is the branch of mathematics devoted to the theory of linear structure, focusing on linear spaces (vector spaces) and linear mappings.
A linear space, or vector space, is a collection of vectors that can be added together and multiplied by scalars, adhering to specific axioms.
A linear mapping is a function between two vector spaces that preserves the operations of vector addition and scalar multiplication.
A linear equation is an equation of the form ax + b = 0, where a and b are constants, and x is a variable, representing a straight line in a geometric context.
The eigenvalue problem involves finding eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix, which are fundamental in understanding linear transformations.
Determinants are scalar values that can be computed from a square matrix, providing important properties such as volume and orientation in geometric contexts.
Gaussian elimination is an algorithm for solving systems of linear equations, transforming matrices into row echelon form.
LU factorization is a method of decomposing a matrix into the product of a lower triangular matrix and an upper triangular matrix, facilitating the solution of linear systems.
QR decomposition is a method of decomposing a matrix into an orthogonal matrix and an upper triangular matrix, useful in solving linear least squares problems.
An eigenvector is a non-zero vector that changes by only a scalar factor when a linear transformation is applied to it.
The residual $r$ is defined as $r = Ax - b$, representing the difference between the observed values $b$ and the values predicted by the model $Ax$.
Normal Equations are a method used to solve the least squares problem, derived from setting the gradient of the residual to zero, although they can be ill-conditioned.
Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is a more general method for solving least squares problems, providing numerical stability and insights into the properties of the matrix $A$.
A quantum group is a mathematical structure that arises in the study of quasi-triangular Hopf algebras and their connection to the quantum Yang-Baxter equations involving the R-matrix.
The quantum Yang-Baxter equation is a fundamental equation in the theory of integrable systems and quantum groups, which plays a crucial role in the formulation of quantum statistical mechanics.
Drinfel'd modules are a generalization of elliptic curves and are used in number theory and algebraic geometry, named after Vladimir Drinfel'd.
Drinfel'd twists are a method for deforming Hopf algebras, allowing for the construction of new algebraic structures that retain certain properties of the original.
For a map $f_{2}: B^{2} \rightarrow N$, the bilinear extension $\varphi_{2}: M^{2} \rightarrow N$ is defined by $(m,n) \mapsto \sum_{b \in B} \sum_{c \in B} m_{b} n_{c} f_{2}(b,c)$.

For any positive integer $n$ and a map $f_{n}: B^{n} \rightarrow N$, the $n$-linear extension $\varphi_{n}: M^{n} \rightarrow N$ is defined by $m \mapsto \sum_{b \in B^{n}} m_{b} f_{n}(b)$, using multi-index notation.
A method for fitting a straight line to a set of data points ${(x_{1},y_{1}),\ldots,(x_{n},y_{n})}$ using a specific formula for the line's parameters.
Defined as $M(a,b) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (ax_{k} + b - y_{k})^{2}$, it quantifies the error of the fit and is minimized to find the best fitting line.
Types of curves that can be fitted to data by transforming the variables, allowing the application of linear least squares fitting techniques.
A diagonal matrix is a matrix in which the entries outside the main diagonal are all zero.
An anti-diagonal matrix is a matrix where all the entries are zero except for the entries on the anti-diagonal.
A triangular matrix is a special kind of square matrix where all the entries either above or below the main diagonal are zero.
Block matrices are matrices that are partitioned into smaller matrices, called blocks, which can be of various forms.
A nilpotent matrix is a matrix for which there exists a positive integer k such that the k-th power of the matrix is the zero matrix.
Elementary matrices are obtained by performing a single elementary row operation on an identity matrix.
Hadamard matrices are square matrices whose entries are either +1 or -1 and whose rows are mutually orthogonal.
Partly decomposable matrices are matrices that can be expressed as a direct sum of simpler matrices.
Fully indecomposable matrices are those that cannot be expressed as a direct sum of two non-trivial matrices.
Nearly decomposable matrices are matrices that are close to being decomposable but have some restrictions.
Doubly stochastic matrices are square matrices where each entry is non-negative and each row and each column sums to one.
Stochastic matrices are matrices used to describe the transitions of a Markov chain, where each row sums to one.
A singular matrix is a square matrix that does not have an inverse, which occurs when its determinant is zero.
Invertible matrices are matrices that have an inverse, meaning there exists another matrix that, when multiplied with it, yields the identity matrix.
Unitary matrices are complex square matrices that are equal to their own conjugate transpose and have a determinant of one.
Orthogonal matrices are square matrices whose rows and columns are orthogonal unit vectors, and their transpose is equal to their inverse.
These are orthogonal matrices that also have a determinant equal to one, representing rotations in n-dimensional space.
Normal matrices are matrices that commute with their conjugate transpose, meaning \(AA^* = A^*A\).
Positive definite matrices are symmetric matrices where all eigenvalues are positive, indicating that the associated quadratic form is positive.
