The classification of composition algebras is a consequence of the theorems established by Hurwitz and Jacobson, which provide foundational results in the study of algebras.
A finite chain is a chain whose cardinality is finite. Any finite set can be made into a chain by inducing a total order from a finite chain.
An infinite chain is a chain that is not finite, meaning it has an infinite cardinality.
A dense chain is a chain such that for every pair of distinct elements $a < b$, there exists an element $c$ such that $a < c < b$. An example is the set of rationals $\mathbb{Q}$.
A Dedekind complete chain is a chain such that every non-empty bounded subset has a supremum and an infimum. An example is the set of reals $\mathbb{R}$.
A chain homomorphism is a function between two chains that preserves the order. It is a poset homomorphism.
A complete chain is a chain such that every subset has a supremum and an infimum. Any finite chain is complete.

Levi's theorem states that for a complex Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ with radical $\mathfrak{r}$, the short exact sequence $0 \rightarrow \mathfrak{r} \rightarrow \mathfrak{g} \rightarrow \mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{r} \rightarrow 0$ is split, implying the existence of a subalgebra $\mathfrak{h}$ of $\mathfrak{g}$ that is isomorphic to $\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{r}$ via the natural projection.

Concept: Regular element of a Lie algebra
Explanation: An element $X \in {\mathfrak{g}}$ is called *regular* if the dimension of its centralizer ${\zeta_{\mathfrak{g}}{(X)}}$ is minimal among all centralizers of elements in $\mathfrak{g}$.
Regular elements are Zariski dense in $\mathfrak{g}$, meaning that they are dense in the Zariski topology, which is a topology used in algebraic geometry.
If $\mathfrak{g}$ is reductive, the minimal centralizer dimension is equal to the rank of $\mathfrak{g}$, which is a measure of the size of the largest abelian subalgebra.

The Killing form on a finite dimensional Lie algebra \( \mathfrak{g} \) is a bilinear map \( B_{\mathfrak{g}}: \mathfrak{g} \times \mathfrak{g} \rightarrow k \) defined by \( B_{\mathfrak{g}}(X,Y) = tr(ad_X \circ ad_Y) \), where \( ad_X \) is the adjoint action.
The adjoint action \( ad_X: \mathfrak{g} \rightarrow \mathfrak{g} \) is defined by \( ad_X Y = [X,Y] \), where \( [X,Y] \) is the Lie bracket of elements \( X \) and \( Y \) in the Lie algebra.

The radical of a Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is the unique maximal solvable ideal of $\mathfrak{g}$. The quotient ${\mathfrak{g}}/{rad}{\mathfrak{g}}$ is semi-simple, meaning it has no solvable ideals.
The real Heisenberg group is defined as the set of upper triangular matrices with ones on the diagonal and real entries in the upper two rows, representing a specific type of Lie group.
A central subgroup is a subgroup that commutes with every element of the group. The subgroup $\Gamma$ in the article is described as central and normal.

Homotopy equivalence is a relation between topological spaces that indicates they have the same 'shape' in a topological sense. The article states that ${SL}_{2}{\mathbb{R}}$ is homotopy equivalent to a circle.
The universal cover of a topological space is a covering space that is simply connected. The article mentions that the universal cover of ${SL}_{2}{\mathbb{R}}$ has an infinite-sheeted cover.

Ado's theorem states that every finite dimensional Lie algebra has a faithful finite dimensional representation, implying that every finite dimensional Lie algebra can be represented as a matrix algebra.
A faithful representation of a Lie algebra is a representation that is injective, meaning that different elements of the algebra are represented by different matrices.

A representation of a Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is a Lie algebra homomorphism $\rho: \mathfrak{g} \rightarrow \operatorname{End} V$, where $\operatorname{End} V$ is the commutator Lie algebra of some vector space $V$. It satisfies $\rho([a,b]) = \rho(a)\rho(b) - \rho(b)\rho(a)$ for all $a, b \in \mathfrak{g}$.
The dimension of $V$ is referred to as the dimension of the representation. If $V$ is infinite-dimensional, it is called an infinite-dimensional representation.

Given two representations $\rho: \mathfrak{g} \rightarrow \operatorname{End}(V)$ and $\sigma: \mathfrak{g} \rightarrow \operatorname{End}(W)$, the direct sum representation is defined on $V \oplus W$ with the Lie algebra action induced by the embeddings of $\operatorname{End}(V) \times \operatorname{End}(W)$ into $\operatorname{End}(V \oplus W)$.

The isotropy representation of a Lie algebra subalgebra ${\mathfrak{h}}$ relative to a Lie algebra ${\mathfrak{g}}$ is the action of ${\mathfrak{h}}$ on the quotient vector space ${\mathfrak{g}}/{\mathfrak{h}}$, defined by the adjoint action of elements in ${\mathfrak{h}}$ on coset representatives.

An invariant form is a bilinear form $B: V 	imes V \rightarrow k$ that satisfies the condition $B(Xv, w) + B(v, Xw) = 0$ for all $X \in \mathfrak{g}$ and $v, w \in V$. This property is significant in the context of Lie algebras.

A representation of a Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ over a field $k$ is a vector space $V$ along with a linear map that associates each element of the Lie algebra with a linear transformation of the vector space, preserving the Lie bracket structure.
The standard Borel subgroup of a subgroup $H \leq G$ consists of all upper triangular matrices that are elements of $H$.
In the context of a complex semi-simple Lie group $G$, a maximal solvable subgroup $B \leq G$ is defined as a Borel subgroup.
A Borel subgroup contains a unique Cartan subgroup, which is a maximal abelian subgroup consisting of diagonalizable elements.
The intersection of a Borel subgroup $B$ with a maximal compact subgroup $K$ of $G$ is the maximal torus of $K$.
A Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is quadratic if it admits a non-degenerate, invariant scalar product $( \cdot \mid \cdot )$ under the adjoint action.

The scalar product $( \cdot \mid \cdot )$ is non-degenerate if the induced map $\phi: {\mathfrak{g}} \rightarrow {\mathfrak{g}}^{*}$ defined by $\phi(X)(Z) = (X \mid Z)$ is an isomorphism of vector spaces.
Invariance of the scalar product means that $({[X,Y]} \mid Z) = - (Y \mid {[X,Z]}) = (Y \mid {[Z,X]})$.
The co-adjoint representation $ad_X^*(\phi(Y)(Z))$ relates the dual space of the Lie algebra to its structure through the scalar product.

A Borel subalgebra $\mathfrak{b}$ is defined as the direct sum of a Cartan subalgebra $\mathfrak{h}$ and the positive root spaces ${\mathfrak{g}}_{\beta}$, where $\beta$ belongs to the set of positive roots $R^{+}$.
A Cartan subalgebra $\mathfrak{h}$ is a maximal abelian subalgebra of a Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$, which plays a crucial role in the structure theory of Lie algebras.
A root system $R$ is a finite set of vectors in a Euclidean space that satisfies certain symmetrical properties, which are essential in the study of Lie algebras.
The set of positive roots $R^{+}$ is a subset of the root system $R$ that is used to define the structure of Borel subalgebras and to classify Lie algebras.
A Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is semi-simple if it can be expressed as a direct sum of simple Lie algebras, which are non-abelian Lie algebras with no non-trivial ideals.

A bilinear form is nondegenerate if the only vector that pairs to zero with all other vectors is the zero vector itself, indicating that the form provides a complete characterization of the vector space.

The Casimir operator is defined as the element $\Omega = \sum{g_{i}g^{i}}$ in the universal enveloping algebra of a semisimple Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$, where $(g_{i}, g^{j}) = \delta_{ij}$ with respect to the Killing form. It is central in the enveloping algebra, meaning it commutes with the action of $\mathfrak{g}$ on any representation.

A representation has highest weight $\lambda$ if there exists a vector $v$ in the weight space $V_{\lambda}$ such that it is annihilated by all elements of the positive root space, making $v$ a highest vector.
The weight space $V_{\lambda}$ associated with a weight $\lambda$ is the subspace of a representation where the action of the Cartan subalgebra corresponds to the eigenvalue $\lambda$.
The weight lattice $\Lambda_{W}$ of a Lie algebra is a lattice formed by the weights of its representations, providing a structure to classify and analyze these weights.

The Cartan matrix of a reduced root system is defined as $C_{i,j} = \left( \frac{2{(\alpha_{i},\alpha_{j})}}{(\alpha_{i},\alpha_{i})} \right)$, where $\Pi = \{\alpha_{1},\cdots,\alpha_{n}\}$ is a base of the root system. It uniquely determines the root system and serves as the basis change matrix from the basis of fundamental weights to the basis of simple roots in the Euclidean vector space $E$.

Dynkin diagrams are combinatorial representations of root systems, facilitating the classification of simple Lie algebras. They consist of vertices representing simple roots and edges indicating the multiplicity of interactions between them.
Simple roots $\Pi$ are a basis for a root system, from which positive roots can be derived, and they form the vertices of the Dynkin diagram.
The Cauchy-Schwartz inequality is a fundamental result in linear algebra that provides a bound on the dot product of two vectors, ensuring that simple roots in a root system are not anti-parallel.
A collection of circles is concentric if they share the same center.
The region formed between two concentric circles.
The cohomology of semi-simple Lie algebras is easier to analyze than that of general Lie algebras due to specific vanishing theorems.
For an irreducible $\mathfrak{g}$-module $M$ of dimension greater than $1$, all cohomology groups with coefficients in $M$ are trivial: $H^{n}({\mathfrak{g},M}) = 0$ for all $n \in {\mathbb{N}}$.
For a finite-dimensional $\mathfrak{g}$-module $M$, the first lemma states that $H^{1}({\mathfrak{g},M}) = 0$ and the second lemma states that $H^{2}({\mathfrak{g},M}) = 0$.
Weyl's theorem states that representations of semi-simple Lie algebras are completely reducible, derived from the vanishing of $H^{1}$.
Indecomposable root systems are classified into four infinite families: $A_n$, $B_n$, $C_n$, and $D_n$, each defined by specific sets of vectors in a Euclidean space.
Root systems are collections of vectors (roots) in a Euclidean space that satisfy certain symmetrical properties, important in the study of Lie algebras and algebraic groups.
$A_n$ is defined as the set of roots $\{\pm e_i \mp e_j : 1 \leq i < j \leq n + 1\}$, where $\mathbf{E}$ is the $(n)$-dimensional subspace perpendicular to $\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i$.
$B_n$ is defined as the union of the roots $\{\pm e_i \pm e_j : 1 \leq i < j \leq n\}$ and the roots $\{\pm e_i : 1 \leq i \leq n\}$.
$C_n$ is defined as the union of the roots $\{\pm e_i \pm e_j : 1 \leq i < j \leq n\}$ and the roots $\{\pm 2e_i : 1 \leq i \leq n\}$.
$D_n$ is defined as the set of roots $\{\pm e_i \pm e_j : 1 \leq i < j \leq n\}$.
There are five exceptional crystallographic root systems: $G_2$, $F_4$, $E_6$, $E_7$, and $E_8$, each defined by specific combinations of the aforementioned root systems.
$G_2$ is defined as $A_3$ combined with additional specific roots involving fractions of standard basis vectors.
$F_4$ is defined as $B_4$ combined with a specific root involving a combination of the standard basis vectors.
$E_6$ is defined as $A_6$ combined with specific roots involving additional vectors and conditions on the number of minus signs.
$E_7$ is defined as $A_8$ combined with specific roots involving a sum of standard basis vectors with conditions on the number of minus signs.
$E_8$ is defined as $D_8$ combined with specific roots involving a sum of standard basis vectors with conditions on the number of minus signs.

The set of roots $R$ corresponds to the eigenvalues of the adjoint action of the Cartan subalgebra on the Lie algebra. Positive roots $R^{+}$ are those whose associated root spaces lie within a Borel subalgebra.

The nilpotent cone $\mathcal{N}$ of a finite dimensional semisimple Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is defined as the set of elements that act nilpotently in all representations of $\mathfrak{g}$. It is given by $\mathcal{N} = \{a \in \mathfrak{g} : \rho(a) \text{ is nilpotent for all representations } \rho : \mathfrak{g} \rightarrow \text{End}(V)\}$. The nilpotent cone is an irreducible subvariety of $\mathfrak{g}$ and is invariant under the adjoint action of $\mathfrak{g}$ on itself.

A semisimple Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is a Lie algebra that is a direct sum of simple Lie algebras, which means it has no nontrivial ideals and is characterized by its representation theory and structure.

An irreducible subvariety is a subset of an algebraic variety that cannot be expressed as the union of two proper closed subsets. The nilpotent cone is an example of such a subvariety in the context of the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$.

The root lattice \( \Lambda_{R} \) of a root system \( R \) is the subset of a Euclidean vector space \( E \) generated by \( R \) as an abelian group, and is free on the simple roots, making it a full sublattice of \( E \).
A root system is called *crystallographic* if for all $u,v \in R$, the expression $2\frac{(u,v)}{(u,u)}$ is an integer.
A root system is called reduced if for all $u \in R$, the only scalars $k$ such that $ku \in R$ are $k = \pm 1$.

A root system is indecomposable if there is no proper decomposition $R = R^{\prime} \cup R^{\operatorname{\prime\prime}}$ where every vector in $R^{\prime}$ is orthogonal to every vector in $R^{\operatorname{\prime\prime}}$.

A subset $R^{+} \subset R$ is called a set of positive roots if there exists a vector $v \in E$ such that $(\alpha,v) > 0$ for all $\alpha \in R^{+}$ and $(\alpha,v) < 0$ for all $\alpha \in R \backslash R^{+}$.

The Chevalley-Serre relations are a set of relations that define a Lie algebra in terms of generators associated with a root system, ensuring the algebra's structure aligns with the properties of the roots.
A Lie algebra is called simple if it has no proper ideals and is not abelian.

Examples include ${\mathfrak{s}}{\mathfrak{l}}_{n}k$, ${\mathfrak{s}}{\mathfrak{o}}_{n}k$, and ${\mathfrak{s}}{\mathfrak{p}}_{2n}k$, which correspond to the special linear, special orthogonal, and symplectic groups respectively.

The representation theory of Lie algebras studies how these algebras can be represented through linear transformations of vector spaces, and it is particularly well understood for simple and semi-simple Lie algebras.
There exists a beautiful classification of simple Lie algebras, which includes infinite series and exceptional algebras.
A synonym for the characteristic function is the indicator function.
A simple root is a positive root that cannot be expressed as the sum of two positive roots, and the collection of simple roots forms a basis for the vector space $E$.

The Verma module $M_{\lambda}$ of weight $\lambda$ is defined as $M_{\lambda} = \mathcal{U}(\mathfrak{g}) \otimes_{\mathcal{U}(\mathfrak{b})} F_{\lambda}$, where $F_{\lambda}$ is a 1-dimensional $\mathfrak{b}$-module. It is infinite dimensional and serves as a universal object among highest weight modules.

A $\mathfrak{g}$-module $V$ is a highest weight module if it has a weight $\mu$ and a non-zero vector $v$ such that $Xv = 0$ for any $X$ in a positive root space, and $V$ is generated by $v$. The Verma module $M_{\lambda}$ is an example of a highest weight module.

This theorem implies that as a vector space, the Verma module $M_{\lambda}$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal{U}(\overline{\mathfrak{n}})$, where $\overline{\mathfrak{n}}$ is the sum of the negative weight spaces.
A representation is said to be diagonalisable if the vector space can be decomposed into a direct sum of weight spaces corresponding to the eigenvalues of the representation.

The positive Weyl chamber is defined as the closed set ${\mathcal{C} = {\{{u \in E} \mid {{(u,\alpha)} \geq {0	ext{~for\ all~}\alpha} \in R^{+}}\}}}$ in a Euclidean vector space $E$, determined by a root system $R$ and a choice of positive roots $R^{+}$.

The reflection in a root $\alpha$ is given by the formula $r_{\alpha}(v) = v - 2\frac{(\alpha,v)}{(\alpha,\alpha)}\alpha$, which reflects the vector $v$ across the hyperplane perpendicular to $\alpha$.

The length function $\ell: W_{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}$ assigns to each element $w$ in the Weyl group the minimal number of reflections in simple roots needed to express $w$. This length also corresponds to the number of positive roots that $w$ transforms into negative roots.
A Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is nilpotent if its lower central series terminates in a trivial ideal, meaning there exists a $k$ such that ${\mathcal{D}_{k}{\mathfrak{g}}} = 0$.

The lower central series of a Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is a descending chain of ideals defined by ${\mathcal{D}_{0}{\mathfrak{g}} = {\mathfrak{g}}}$ and ${\mathcal{D}_{k + 1}{\mathfrak{g}} = {\lbrack{\mathfrak{g},{\mathcal{D}_{k}{\mathfrak{g}}}\rbrack}}$.
Engel's theorem states that if $g$ is a Lie algebra of nilpotent transformations on a finite-dimensional vector space $V$, then $g$ is a nilpotent Lie algebra.
The joint kernel ${\ker{\mathfrak{g}} = {\bigcap\limits_{a \in {\mathfrak{g}}}{\ker a}}}$ is the intersection of the kernels of all endomorphisms in the Lie algebra $g$.

The upper central series of a Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is an ascending series of subalgebras defined by $\mathcal{D}^{1}{\mathfrak{g}} = [\mathfrak{g}, \mathfrak{g}]$ and $\mathcal{D}^{k}{\mathfrak{g}} = [\mathcal{D}^{k - 1}{\mathfrak{g}}, \mathcal{D}^{k - 1}{\mathfrak{g}}]$ for $k \geq 2$.
A Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is solvable if its upper central series $\mathcal{D}^{k}{\mathfrak{g}} = 0$ for some natural number $k$.

A universal enveloping algebra of a Lie algebra \( \mathfrak{g} \) over a field \( k \) is an associative algebra \( U \) with unity, along with a Lie algebra homomorphism \( \iota: \mathfrak{g} \rightarrow U \), satisfying a specific universal property related to other associative algebras.
The fundamental operations of arithmetic include addition ($+$), subtraction ($-$), multiplication ($	imes$), and division ($\div$), which are essential for performing calculations.
A mapping \( f: A \rightarrow B \) is surjective if for every element \( y \in B \), there exists an element \( x \in A \) such that \( f(x) = y \).

The extended Cartan matrix $\hat{A}$ is derived from the Cartan matrix $A$ by adding a zero-th row and column corresponding to a new simple root $\alpha_{0}:={- 	heta}$, where $	heta$ is the maximal root of $\mathfrak{g}$.
The Kac-Moody Lie algebra associated with the extended Cartan matrix $\hat{A}$ is said to be of affine type, indicating a generalization of finite-dimensional Lie algebras.
The maximal root $	heta$ is defined as a root of $\mathfrak{g}$ that, when expressed in terms of simple roots, has the maximal sum of coefficients, indicating its height.
Ordered monomials are products of elements from a basis of a Lie algebra that are arranged in a specific order according to a linear ordering.

A simultaneous eigenvector for a set of operators (or elements of a Lie algebra) is a non-zero vector that is an eigenvector for each operator in the set, meaning it satisfies the eigenvalue equation for each operator.

A generalized Cartan matrix is a matrix $A$ with diagonal entries equal to 2 and off-diagonal entries that are nonpositive integers, satisfying the condition that $a_{ij} = 0$ if and only if $a_{ji} = 0$.
A generalized Cartan matrix is called symmetrizable if there exists a diagonal matrix $B$ such that the product $AB$ is symmetric.

The Kac-Moody algebra associated to a generalized Cartan matrix $A$ is a Lie algebra generated by elements $X_{1},\ldots,X_{n},Y_{1},\ldots,Y_{n}$ and elements of a complex vector space $\mathfrak{h}$, defined by specific relations involving roots and coroots.

Roots ${\alpha_{1},\ldots,\alpha_{n}}$ are linearly independent elements in the dual space ${\mathfrak{h}}^{*}$, while coroots ${\check{\alpha}}_{1},\ldots,{\check{\alpha}}_{n}$ are elements in ${\mathfrak{h}}$, related through the natural pairing ${\langle\alpha_{i},\check{\alpha_{j}}\rangle} = a_{ij}$.
Any vector space can be made into a Lie algebra by setting $[x,y] = 0$ for all vectors $x,y$, resulting in an abelian Lie algebra.
${\mathbb{R}}^{3}$ with the cross product operation is an example of a nonabelian three-dimensional Lie algebra over $\mathbb{R}$.

An associative envelope of a Lie algebra is an associative algebra in which the Lie algebra can be embedded, allowing for the study of the Lie algebra's structure within the context of associative algebras.
The associator bracket is defined for elements a, b, and c in a non-associative algebra as [a, b, c] = ((ab)c - a(bc)), which is used to explore the Jacobi identity in non-associative contexts.
A conjunction is a logical operation that results in true only when both of its operands (conjuncts) are true. It is represented symbolically as $a \land b$, $a \cdot b$, or $ab$.

The Local Nagano Theorem states that for a Lie subalgebra of real analytic vector fields, there exists a real analytic submanifold containing a point such that the tangent space at that point corresponds to the Lie subalgebra.
The germ of a manifold at a point is an equivalence class of functions that agree on some neighborhood of that point, capturing the local behavior of the manifold.
The local Nagano leaf of a Lie subalgebra at a point $x_{0}$ is defined as the germ of the manifold $M$ at that point.
The global Nagano leaf is the union of all connected real analytic embedded submanifolds whose germ at a point coincides with that of the local Nagano leaf.

A real Lie group $K$ is a group that is a differentiable manifold and has a group structure that is compatible with the manifold structure. It is called a real form of a complex Lie group $G$ if its Lie algebra can be expressed in terms of the Lie algebra of $G$ as ${\mathfrak{g}} \cong {{\mathbb{C}} \otimes_{\mathbb{R}}{\mathfrak{k}}}$.

A complex Lie group $G$ is a group that is a complex manifold and has a group structure that is compatible with the complex manifold structure. It is characterized by its complex Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$.
A representation of a group or algebra into $GL(V,k)$, $End_{k}V$, or ${\mathfrak{g}}{\mathfrak{l}}_{k}(V)$ is called *modular* if the characteristic of the field $k$ is positive.
A theorem that fails in modular representations, which typically relies on rational fractions that can cause division by zero in characteristic $p$.
A theorem that does not hold in modular representations due to issues with inseparable polynomials and repeated roots in positive characteristic fields.
A canonical form for linear transformations that can change in modular representations due to inseparable polynomials.

The unitary Lie algebra ${\mathfrak{u}}{(V,B)}$ consists of linear transformations $T$ on a vector space $V$ that satisfy the condition ${B{({Tx},y)} + {B{(x,{Ty})}} = 0}$ for all ${x,y} \in V$, where $B$ is a hermitian form relative to an involution $\sigma$.
The special unitary Lie algebra ${\mathfrak{s}}u{(V,B)}$ consists of those linear transformations in the unitary Lie algebra ${\mathfrak{u}}{(V,B)}$ that have trace zero.

A modular Lie algebra $L$ is *restricted* if there exists a *power map* ${\lbrack p\rbrack}: L \rightarrow L$ satisfying specific conditions related to the adjoint representation and linear transformations.
The *power map* ${\lbrack p\rbrack}$ is a function that relates elements of the Lie algebra to their powers, capturing properties of the field in the context of positive characteristic.
A Lie algebra is *restrictable* if it can be endowed with a power mapping that qualifies it as a restricted Lie algebra.
A special Jordan algebra is one where the product is defined as $x.{y = {\frac{1}{2}{({{xy} + {yx}})}}}$. This allows for specific computations of the Jordan triple product.
A quadratic Jordan algebra is a structure where the Jordan triple product plays a role as one of the two quadratic operations, alongside the usual squaring product $y\mapsto y^{.2}$.
The Jordan identity is given by ${(a^{.2}.b)}.a = a^{.2}.{(b.a)}$, establishing a connection between the triple product and the quadratic unary product.

The Jacobi product on an algebra with a $\lbrack,\rbrack$ multiplication is defined as ${{\# xyz\#} = {{\lbrack{\lbrack x,y\rbrack},z\rbrack} + {\lbrack{\lbrack y,z\rbrack},x\rbrack} + {\lbrack{\lbrack z,x\rbrack},y\rbrack}}}$, which contrasts with the Jordan triple product.
A Jordan homomorphism from a Jordan algebra $A$ to another Jordan algebra $B$ is an algebra homomorphism that respects the commutative law and the Jordan identity.
A Jordan algebra isomorphism is a bijective Jordan algebra homomorphism between two Jordan algebras.
The Jordan product is defined for an associative algebra $A$ with the new multiplication $ab = \frac{1}{2}(a \cdot b + b \cdot a)$, making $A$ a Jordan algebra.
A Jordan algebra that is not isomorphic to any Jordan subalgebra of an associative algebra $A^{+}$.
An inner quadratic ideal \( I \) of a quadratic Jordan algebra \( J \) is a submodule such that for all \( i \in I \) and \( x \in J \), the element \( U_{i}(x) \) belongs to \( I \).
An outer quadratic ideal \( I \) of a quadratic Jordan algebra \( J \) is a submodule such that for all \( i \in I \) and \( x \in J \), the element \( U_{x}(i) \) belongs to \( I \).
The zeroth K-group of a ring R, denoted K_0(R), is the Grothendieck group of idempotents in the algebraic direct limit of matrix algebras M_{∞}(R) up to similarity transformations.
The first K-group of a ring R, denoted K_1(R), is the abelianisation of the direct limit of general linear groups GL_{∞}(R).
The second K-group of a ring R, denoted K_2(R), is the second group homology group of the direct limit of the elementary subgroup E_{∞}(R) of GL_{∞}(R).
Higher K-groups are defined using the Quillen plus construction, specifically K_{n}^{alg}(R) = π_{n}(BGL_{∞}(R)^{+}).
Algebraic K-theory has a product structure given by a map from K_{i}(R) ⊗ K_{j}(S) to K_{i+j}(R ⊗ S).

A non-associative algebra $A$ is *alternative* if it satisfies the left alternative laws ${[a,a,b]} = 0$ and the right alternative laws ${[b,a,a]} = 0$ for any $a,b \in A$, where $[,,]$ is the associator on $A$.
In an alternative algebra, the associator is *anti-commutative*, meaning $[a,b,c] = -[b,a,c]$, $[a,b,c] = -[a,c,b]$, and $[a,b,c] = -[c,b,a]$ when one of the three coordinates is held fixed.
Artin's Theorem states that a non-associative algebra $A$ is alternative if and only if every subalgebra generated by two elements is associative, provided $A$ is not Boolean.
An alternative ring is a non-associative ring that satisfies the conditions ${({aa})b} = a({ab})$ and ${({ab})b} = a({bb})$ for any $a,b \in R$, equivalently an alternative algebra over $\mathbb{Z}$.
A permutation is classified as odd if it has an odd number of even-order cycles in its cycle type.
A permutation is classified as even if it has an even number of even-order cycles in its cycle type.
The cycle type of a permutation refers to the decomposition of the permutation into disjoint cycles, which can be analyzed to determine the permutation's signature.
A transposition is a permutation that swaps two elements, and can be used to express cycles as products of transpositions.
Algebraic K-theory groups, denoted as $K_n(R)$ for a ring $R$, are a sequence of abelian groups that provide invariants of the ring related to projective modules and vector bundles.
$K_0(R)$ is the group of isomorphism classes of projective modules over the ring $R$, providing a measure of the 'size' of the projective module category.
$K_1(R)$ is related to the group of units of the ring $R$, specifically capturing information about the invertible elements in $R$.
$K_2(R)$ is associated with higher algebraic structures and can be interpreted in terms of certain relations among projective modules.
$K_3(R)$ is a higher K-theory group that provides additional invariants and is often studied in relation to the topology of vector bundles.
$K_4(R)$ is another higher K-theory group that can be related to the structure of certain algebraic objects, although it is often trivial for many rings.
A generalised cohomology theory that classifies vector bundles over compact Hausdorff spaces up to stable equivalences.
A theorem stating that K-groups exhibit periodicity, specifically that K_{1}(SA) is isomorphic to K_{0}(A).
Groups defined through repeated suspensions, denoted as K_{n}(A) = K_{0}(S^{n}A).
A relation between two projections or unitaries where they can be transformed into each other via unitary operators.
A property of K-groups that states K_{i}(A ⊗ K) is isomorphic to K_{i}(A) for a compact operator algebra K.
The property that K-groups remain unchanged under Morita equivalence, specifically K_{i}(M_{n}(A)) = K_{i}(A).
A variant of K-theory that incorporates a real structure, exhibiting a Bott period of 8.
K-theory for complex algebras, denoted as K_{i}(C(X, C)), related to unitary bundles.
Two left $R$-modules $M$ and $N$ are stably isomorphic if there exists a finitely generated free $R$-module $R^{n}$ such that $M \oplus R^{n} \cong N \oplus R^{n}$.
A left $R$-module $M$ is stably free if it is stably isomorphic to a finitely generated free $R$-module, i.e., $M \oplus R^{m} \cong R^{n}$ for some positive integers $m,n$.

K-homology is a homology theory on the category of compact Hausdorff spaces that classifies elliptic pseudo-differential operators acting on vector bundles over a space, and in terms of $C^{*}$-algebras, it classifies Fredholm modules over an algebra.
Fredholm modules are algebraic structures used in K-homology, where an operator homotopy between two Fredholm modules is a norm continuous path of Fredholm modules.
The $K^{0}(A)$ group is the abelian group of equivalence classes of even Fredholm modules over a $C^{*}$-algebra $A$.
The $K^{1}(A)$ group is the abelian group of equivalence classes of odd Fredholm modules over a $C^{*}$-algebra $A$.

K-theory groups, denoted as $K_{0}(A)$ and $K_{1}(A)$, are algebraic invariants associated with a topological space or a $C^*$-algebra $A$. They provide information about vector bundles and the structure of the algebra.
$K_{0}(A)$ is the group that classifies vector bundles over a space or $C^*$-algebra $A$, capturing the notion of stable isomorphism of vector bundles.
$K_{1}(A)$ is the group that classifies the unitary elements of a $C^*$-algebra $A$, related to the fundamental group of the unitary group of the algebra.

$C^*$-algebras are a class of algebras that arise in functional analysis, characterized by the property that they are closed under the operation of taking adjoints and are complete with respect to a certain norm.
Fredholm modules represent abstract elliptic pseudo-differential operators and can be classified as odd or even based on their structure.

An odd Fredholm module $(\mathcal{H},F)$ consists of an involutive representation $\pi$ of a $C^{*}$-algebra $A$ on a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$, with an operator $F$ satisfying $F = F^{*}$, $F^{2} = {1I}$, and ${\lbrack F,\pi{(a)}\rbrack} \in {\mathbb{K}(\mathcal{H})}$ for all $a \in A$.

An even Fredholm module $(\mathcal{H},F,\Gamma)$ is an odd Fredholm module augmented with a ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$-grading $\Gamma$ on $\mathcal{H}$ that satisfies specific commutation relations with $\pi(a)$ and $F$.
A Fredholm module is degenerate if ${\lbrack F,\pi{(a)}\rbrack} = 0$ for all $a \in A$, indicating that it is homotopic to the 0-module.

Concept: Example of Fredholm module over C
Explanation: An even Fredholm module $(H,F,\Gamma)$ over $C$ is illustrated with specific matrices for $\mathcal{H}$, $F$, and $\Gamma$ that satisfy the definitions of even Fredholm modules.
The canonical divisor $K$ is a divisor that encodes the differential forms on the curve, with its dimension given by $\ell{(K)} = g$.
The Steinberg group $St{(R)}$ describes the minimal amount of relations between elementary matrices in an associative ring $R$ with identity.
The kernel of the map $\left. {St{(R)}}\rightarrow{E{(R)}} \right.$ is the second algebraic $K$-group of the ring $R$, denoted as $K_{2}{(R)}$.
The Steinberg group is the universal central extension of the group of elementary matrices $E{(R)}$.
The alternating group $A_{n}$ is defined as the group of even permutations of $n$ elements. It is a normal subgroup of the symmetric group $S_{n}$.
A near ring $(R, +, \cdot)$ is an algebraic structure that has a multiplicative identity and where multiplication left distributes over addition.

A function is a one-to-one correspondence (bijective) if every element of one set is paired with exactly one element of another set, and vice versa. The function $\pi$ establishes a one-to-one correspondence between $S_{n} \smallsetminus A_{n}$ and $A_{n}$.

Even permutations are those that can be expressed as a product of an even number of transpositions, while odd permutations can be expressed as a product of an odd number of transpositions. The relationship between even and odd permutations is crucial in the proof.

An operation is non-associative if there exist elements $a, b, c$ such that $\phi(a, \phi(b, c)) 
eq \phi(\phi(a, b), c)$, as demonstrated with the operation $
u(a, b) = a^2 + b$.
The property of being associative can be extended to $n$-ary operations, where an $n$-ary operation $\phi$ is associative if the equality holds for any arrangement of its inputs.

The Bruhat decomposition states that the quotient of the coset space $B \backslash G / B$ is isomorphic to the Weyl group $W$, where $G$ is a reductive group and $B$ is a Borel subgroup. This can also be expressed as $G = BWB$.
A $p$-group is a finite group of order $p^{n}$, where $p$ is a prime number and $n$ is a positive integer. Every $p$-group has a non-trivial center.
A binary operation $\circ$ on a set $S$ is commutative if for all $a,b \in S$, it holds that $a \circ b = b \circ a$. This can also be expressed as $\circ(a,b) = \circ(b,a)$.

An $n$-ary operation $f$ on a set $A$ is commutative if for every permutation $\pi$ of $\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ and for every choice of $n$ elements $a_i$ of $A$, it holds that $f(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n) = f(a_{\pi(1)}, a_{\pi(2)}, \ldots, a_{\pi(n)})$.
An equivalence relation on a group $G$ that is compatible with the group structure, meaning it preserves the group operation and the operation of taking inverses.
The cube of a number $x$ is defined as the third power of $x$, denoted as $x^{3}$.
The volume of a cube with side length $a$ is given by the formula $a^{3}$.
The cube function is defined as the mapping $x \mapsto x^{3}$ from $\mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{R}$, which is injective.
The cube function is not injective when considered as a mapping from $\mathbb{C}$ to $\mathbb{C}$, as multiple values can yield the same cube.
In the context of the cube function, the third root of unity is expressed as $\frac{-1 \pm i\sqrt{3}}{2}$, indicating values of $x$ and $y$ for which $x^{3} = y^{3}$.
A free group is a group with no relations other than those required by the group axioms, consisting of all finite strings formed from a set of generators and their inverses.
The monster group is the largest of the sporadic simple groups, containing approximately $8 \cdot 10^{53}$ elements, and is a central object of study in group theory.

A set $X$ is called a left $G$-Set if there exists a mapping $\lambda: G \times X \rightarrow X$ satisfying $\lambda(g_1, \lambda(g_2, x)) = \lambda(g_1 g_2, x)$ for all $x \in X$ and $g_1, g_2 \in G$. This can be expressed more simply as $\lambda(g, x) = gx$.

A set $X$ is called a right $G$-Set if there exists a mapping $\lambda: X \times G \rightarrow X$ such that $\lambda(\lambda(x, g_2), g_1) = \lambda(x, g_2 g_1)$ for all $x \in X$ and $g_1, g_2 \in G$.
The power laws are defined as $a^{m} \cdot a^{n} = a^{m+n}$ and $(a^{m})^{n} = a^{mn}$, which hold for positive integer exponents under an associative operation.
The power of product law states that $(a \cdot b)^{n} = a^{n} \cdot b^{n}$, which requires the operation to be commutative.
For zero and negative integer exponents, the definitions are $a^{0} := e$ (the neutral element) and $a^{-n} := (a^{-1})^{n}$, extending the power notion.
Fractional exponents are introduced by using roots, allowing the extension of the power notion beyond integer exponents.
Irrational exponents are defined using limits of sequences of rational numbers, allowing for the extension of exponentiation to irrational numbers.
Imaginary exponents extend the concept of exponentiation to non-real complex numbers, leading to the definition of the general power.

The subring generated by a nonempty subset $M$ of a ring $A$ is the smallest subring of $A$ that includes $M$, denoted by $\left\langle M \right\rangle$. It consists of finite sums of monomials formed by elements of $M$.
The subring generated by $M$ is formed by finite sums of monomials of the form $a_{1}a_{2}\cdots a_{n}$, where $a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n} \in M$.

Concept: Inverse of an element in a group
Explanation: The inverse of an element $a$ in a group $(G,*)$ is denoted as $a^{-1}$, satisfying the equation $a * a^{-1} = a^{-1} * a = e$, where $e$ is the identity element of the group.
The statement ${(a^{-1})}^{-1} = a$ asserts that taking the inverse of the inverse of an element in a group returns the original element.
The identity element $e$ in a group $(G,*)$ is defined such that for any element $a \in G$, the equations $a * e = a$ and $e * a = a$ hold true.
The Klein 4-ring contains subrings $\{ 0,a\}$, $\{ 0,b\}$, and $\{ 0,c\}$, which are subsets of the ring that themselves form rings under the operations defined.
For an element $a$ in a set $S$ and a positive integer $n$, the expression $a^n$ denotes the product of $n$ equal factors of $a$.
For an element $a$ in a set $S$ and a positive integer $n$, the expression $na$ denotes the sum of $n$ equal elements of $a$.
In a ring \( (R, + , \cdot) \), an element \( e \) is called the left unity if it satisfies the equation \( e \cdot a = a \) for all \( a \in R \).
In a ring \( R \), an element \( e' \) is called the right unity if it satisfies the equation \( a \cdot e' = a \) for all \( a \in R \).

If a ring \( R \) has both a left unity \( e \) and a right unity \( e' \), they must coincide, meaning every right unity equals \( e \) and every left unity equals \( e \). This is referred to simply as the unity of the ring.
An indecomposable group is a nontrivial group that cannot be expressed as the internal direct product of two proper normal subgroups.
A group that is not indecomposable is called decomposable.
In a ring, multiplication is a binary operation that combines two elements to produce another element, and it must satisfy certain properties such as associativity and distributivity over addition.
The Minkowski sum satisfies properties such as commutativity ($A + B = B + A$) and scalar multiplication ($\lambda(A + B) = \lambda A + \lambda B$).
A decomposable ring is a ring that can be expressed as a direct sum of two nontrivial unital rings, denoted as $R = R_{1} \oplus R_{2}$.
The mapping $f_{n}$ is defined recursively for $n \geq 2$ such that $f_{n}(a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n})$ is the union of products of subsets formed by previous mappings.

If $A$ is an ideal in a ring $R$ and $S$ is a subring of $R$, then $S + A$ is a subring of $R$, $A$ is an ideal in $S + A$, $S \cap A$ is an ideal in $S$, and there is an isomorphism $S/{(S \cap A)} \cong (S + A)/A$.

A non-commutative ring is a ring in which the multiplication operation is not commutative; that is, for some elements \( x \) and \( y \) in the ring, \( xy 
eq yx \).
The property of an equivalence relation where if $a \sim b$, then $b \sim a$. This is demonstrated by showing that if $ab^{-1} \in H$, then $ba^{-1} \in H$.
The elements of a subset $X$ are called *generators* of the submodule $RX$, which is defined as the submodule generated by $X$.
Given submodules $A$ and $B$ of $T$, their sum is defined as $A + B = \{a + b \in T : a \in A \land b \in B\}$, which is also a submodule of $T$.
The intersection $A \cap B$ of submodules $A$ and $B$ is a submodule of $T$.
The quotient of left $R$-submodules $A$ and $B$ of $T$ is defined as $[A:B] = \{ t \in T : tB \subseteq A \}$, which is also a submodule of $T$.
In a group $(G, \ast)$, every element $g$ has a unique inverse $h$ such that $g \ast h = h \ast g = e$, where $e$ is the identity element.
A point $a$ is a discontinuity point of the characteristic function $\chi_{A}$ if any neighborhood of $a$ contains points $b$ and $c$ such that $\chi_{A}(b) = 1$ and $\chi_{A}(c) = 0$.
A point $a$ is a boundary point of a set $A$ if every neighborhood of $a$ contains points from both $A$ and its complement, indicating that $a$ is on the edge of $A$.
The zero element $0$ in a ring $R$ is the additive identity, meaning that for any element $a \in R$, $a + 0 = a$.
A group \( G \) is said to have even order if the number of elements in \( G \), denoted \( |G| \), is an even integer.

An element \( b \) in a group \( G \) is of order 2 if \( b 
eq e \) (the identity element) and \( b^2 = e \), meaning that \( b \) is its own inverse.
The center of a group is a characteristic subgroup, which means it is invariant under all automorphisms of the group.
The center consists of conjugacy classes that contain just one element, indicating that elements in the center are not conjugate to any other element.
For every prime $p$, every non-trivial finite $p$-group has a non-trivial center, which is a significant property in group theory.
The quotient $G/Z(G)$ is called the central quotient of $G$, and it is isomorphic to the inner automorphism group $Inn(G)$.
An operator is a mapping, function, or transformation, particularly in linear algebra, where a transformation $T: V \rightarrow W$ is called an operator on $V$ if $W = V$.
A $K$--valued class function on a group $G$ is a function $f: G \longrightarrow K$ such that $f(g) = f(h)$ whenever $g$ and $h$ are elements of the same conjugacy class of $G$.

An important example of a class function, where the set of characters of the irreducible representations of $G$ forms a basis for the vector space of all $\mathbb{C}$--valued class functions when $G$ is a compact Lie group.
Class functions are also known as central functions because they are central elements of the convolution algebra $C^{*}(G)$, which commute with all other functions in the algebra.
If $G$ has only one subgroup of a given cardinality, then that subgroup is characteristic, meaning it is invariant under all automorphisms of $G$.
The property of normality is not transitive; if $K{{char}H}$ and $H	rianglelefteq G$, it does not imply that $K	rianglelefteq G$.
The cyclic group of order mn, where m and n are relatively prime.
The cyclic group of order m, generated by the element x.
The cyclic group of order n, generated by the element y.
The external direct product of the cyclic groups C_{m} and C_{n}, consisting of elements (x^{i}, y^{j}).

Two elements $g$ and $g^{\prime}$ of a group $G$ are conjugate if there exists $h \in G$ such that $g^{\prime} = hgh^{-1}$. This relation is an equivalence relation, leading to equivalence classes known as conjugacy classes.
Two subsets $S$ and $T$ of $G$ are conjugate if there exists $g \in G$ such that $T = \{gsg^{-1} \mid s \in S\}$. This is often denoted as $gSg^{-1}$.
The stabilizer subgroup $G_{\alpha}$ of an element $\alpha \in M$ is the set of all elements in $G$ that fix $\alpha$ under the group action.

The core of a subgroup $H$ in a group $G$ is defined as the intersection of all conjugates of $H$ in $G$, denoted as ${core}_{G}(H) = \bigcap_{x \in G}{x^{-1}Hx}$. It represents the largest normal subgroup of $G$ contained in $H$.
A subgroup $H$ is said to be core-free if its core ${core}_{G}(H) = \{ 1 \}$, meaning it does not contain any nontrivial normal subgroups of $G$.
A subgroup $H$ is normal-by-finite if its core ${core}_{G}(H)$ is of finite index in $H$, indicating that the quotient $H/{core}_{G}(H)$ is finite.
A subgroup $H$ has finite index in a group $G$ if the number of left cosets of $H$ in $G$, denoted $[G:H]$, is finite.
A group is virtually abelian if it contains an abelian subgroup of finite index, which implies that it has a structure closely related to abelian groups.
The left coset of an element $a$ with respect to a subgroup $H$ in a group $G$ is defined as the set $aH = \{ah \mid h \in H\}$.
The right coset of an element $a$ with respect to a subgroup $H$ in a group $G$ is defined as the set $Ha = \{ha \mid h \in H\}$.
The collection of left cosets (or right cosets) partitions the group $G$, meaning that the cosets are either identical or disjoint.
In mathematical statements, a *fixed* object is one that does not vary throughout the statement or proof, although it is typically chosen arbitrarily.
The term *fixes* in the context of mappings indicates that a mapping sends a mathematical object to itself, which is a property of the mapping.
An $(H,K)$-double coset is a set of the form $HxK$ for some $x \in G$, defined as $HxK = \{hxk \mid h \in H \text{ and } k \in K\}$. It is a union of right cosets of $H$ and left cosets of $K$.
The $(H,\{1\})$-double cosets are just the right cosets of $H$.
The $(\{1\},K)$-double cosets are just the left cosets of $K$.

A map $f: X \rightarrow Y$ is called equivariant if it satisfies the condition $g(f(x)) = f(gx)$ for all $g \in G$ when $X$ and $Y$ are left homogeneous spaces, or $f(x)g = f(xg)$ for right homogeneous spaces.
The Feit-Thompson theorem states that every non-Abelian simple group must have even order.
The conjecture states that there are no prime numbers $p$ and $q$ (with $p \neq q$) such that $\frac{p^{q} - 1}{p - 1}$ is divisible by $\frac{q^{p} - 1}{q - 1}$.
A non-empty finite subset $K$ of a group $G$ is a subgroup of $G$ if and only if for all $x, y \in K$, the product $xy$ is also in $K$.

Finite simple groups are groups that are nontrivial and do not have any normal subgroups other than the trivial group and the group itself. Their classification is believed to be complete, with a proof spanning over 10,000 pages.

The alternating group \( A_n \) is the group of all even permutations of \( n \) symbols, and it is a normal subgroup of the symmetric group \( S_n \). It is simple for \( n \geq 5 \) and plays a crucial role in Galois theory.
Groups of Lie type are a class of finite groups that arise from algebraic groups over finite fields. They include projective special linear groups and other related groups.

Sporadic groups are a set of twenty-six exceptional groups that do not fit into any of the infinite families of simple groups. They often arise in the context of automorphisms of strongly regular graphs.

The Fourth Isomorphism Theorem states that there is a bijection between the set of subgroups of a group $G$ containing a normal subgroup $N$ and the set of subgroups of the quotient group $G/N$. This theorem establishes several important relationships between subgroups of $G$ and their corresponding subgroups in $G/N$.
The area of a trapezium is calculated using the formula \( \frac{1}{2} \times \text{Sum of parallel sides} \times \text{Distance between parallel sides} \).
The area of a rectangle is given by the formula \( \text{Length} \times \text{Breadth} \).
The perimeter of a rectangle can be calculated using the formula \( 2 \times (\text{Length} + \text{Breadth}) \).
The area of a square is determined by the formula \( \text{Side} \times \text{Side} \).
The perimeter of a square is calculated using the formula \( 4 \times \text{Side} \).
The area of a general quadrilateral can be calculated using the formula \( \frac{1}{2} \times d \times (h_1 + h_2) \), where \( d \) is the diameter and \( h_1 \) and \( h_2 \) are the heights.
The area of a triangle is given by the formula \( \frac{1}{2} \times \text{breadth} \times \text{height} \).
The perimeter of a triangle is the sum of the lengths of all three sides.
The area of a quadrilateral is calculated using the formula \( 4 \times \text{Side} \times \text{Side} \).
The area of a rhombus is given by the formula \( \frac{1}{2} \times D_1 \times D_2 \), where \( D_1 \) and \( D_2 \) are the lengths of the diagonals.
A group that has a generating set with only finitely many elements is called a finitely generated group.
The subgroup of $G$ generated by a subset $X$, denoted by $\langle X \rangle$, is the smallest subgroup of $G$ that contains $X$.
The generating rank of $G$ is the minimum cardinality of a generating set of $G$. If $G$ is uncountable, its generating rank is simply $|G|$.
The Schreier index formula provides a bound on the number of generators required for a subgroup of finite index in a finitely generated group.
Finitely generated groups all of whose subgroups are also finitely generated satisfy the maximal condition, which includes all finitely generated nilpotent groups and polycyclic groups.
A group that is not finitely generated is sometimes referred to as an infinitely generated group.

The orbit of an element $x \in X$ under the action of a group $G$ is the set of elements that can be reached by the action of all elements of $G$ on $x$, denoted as $G(x) = \{ g \cdot x \mid g \in G \}$.

A group action is said to be faithful if different elements of the group induce different bijective maps on the set, meaning that the only element that fixes every point in $X$ is the identity element $1_G$. This is equivalent to the kernel of the homomorphism being trivial, $\ker(F) = \{ 1_G \}$.
A group in which the group operation is not commutative; there exist elements a and b such that a * b ≠ b * a.
A group that is both abelian and has every non-identity element of order p, where p is a prime number.
The group of symmetries of a regular polygon, including rotations and reflections; denoted as D_n for a polygon with n sides.
A group that generalizes the quaternion group, often denoted as Dic(C_n) for a cyclic group of order n.
The inner automorphism group ${Inn}(G)$ is defined as the group of automorphisms of $G$ that are given by conjugation by elements of $G$. It is isomorphic to the central quotient ${G/Z(G)}$.
The Klein four-group $V_{4}$ is an abelian group of order 4, which can be represented as the group of order 4 formed by the cosets of the center $Z(G)$ in the dihedral group ${\mathcal{D}_{8}}$.

A homogeneous group is a set $G$ with a map ${()}: G \times G \times G \rightarrow G$ satisfying specific properties that allow the definition of a product on $G$ and the existence of homomorphisms between such groups.

A homogeneous space is a set $X$ on which a group $G$ acts transitively, meaning that for any two elements in $X$, there exists an element in $G$ that maps one to the other. This implies that the elements of $X$ are indistinguishable under the action of $G$.

A group action of $G$ on a set $X$ is called transitive if for any two elements $x, y \in X$, there exists an element $g \in G$ such that $g \cdot x = y$. This means the action can move any point to any other point in the set.
The isotropy subgroup $H_x$ at a point $x \in X$ is the set of all elements in $G$ that fix $x$ under the group action. It captures the symmetries of the space at the basepoint.

An equivariant bijection between two homogeneous spaces $G/H_0$ and $G/H_1$ is a bijective map that commutes with the group actions on both spaces. This means that the structure of the group action is preserved under the mapping.

A parametre is a quantity considered constant in a specific situation but may take different values in others; it acts as a 'variable constant'. In parametric equations, parametres serve as auxiliary variables that determine the coordinates of points.

The parametric equations for a circle centered at the origin are given by \( x = a \cos t \) and \( y = a \sin t \), where \( a \) is the radius (a variable constant) and \( t \) is an auxiliary variable that takes all real values from the interval \( [0, 2\pi] \).

In analytic geometry, the parametre of a parabola, also known as the latus rectum, is the chord perpendicular to the axis that passes through the focus, represented by the quantity \( 2p \) in the standard equation \( x^2 = 2py \), where \( p \) is the distance from the focus to the directrix.
Cycle decomposition is the process of expressing a permutation as a product of disjoint cycles.
An element \( x \) in a group has finite order if there exists a positive integer \( n \) such that \( n \cdot x = 0 \), where \( 0 \) is the identity element of the group.

The additive group of a ring \( R \) consists of the elements of \( R \) under the operation of addition. It is a group where the operation is addition and the identity element is the additive identity (0).

The homomorphic image of a group is a group. Specifically, if \( f \) is a homomorphism from the group \( (G, \ast) \) to the groupoid \( (\Gamma, \star) \), then the groupoid \( (f(G), \star) \) is also a group.
An automorphism of a group $G$ that corresponds to conjugation by some element $x \in G$, defined by the mapping $\phi_{x}(y) = xyx^{-1}$ for $y \in G$.
An automorphism of a group $G$ that is not an inner automorphism.
The kernel of the surjective group homomorphism from $G$ to $Inn(G)$, denoted as $Z(G)$, which consists of elements that commute with all elements of $G$.
A mapping from group $G$ to $Inn(G)$ that is both a homomorphism and surjective, with kernel $Z(G)$.

Two groups $(X_{1},*_{1})$ and $(X_{2},*_{2})$ are isomorphic if there exists a group isomorphism $\psi: X_{1} \rightarrow X_{2}$. Isomorphic groups have the same structure, meaning they are 'abstractly identical'.
If one group is cyclic, the other must also be cyclic. If $X_{1}$ is generated by an element $g$, then $X_{2}$ is generated by $\psi(g)$.

The inverse of the product of two elements $a$ and $b$ in a group is given by $({a*b})^{-1} = {b^{-1}*a^{-1}}$. This means that to find the inverse of a product, you reverse the order of the elements and take their individual inverses.
The neutral element $e$ of a group is the unique element such that for any element $a$ in the group, the equation $a * e = a$ and $e * a = a$ holds.

In the context of double cosets, the proposition states that the unions of right and left cosets within a double coset can be expressed as disjoint unions, meaning that the elements of these unions do not overlap.

The mapping \( \varphi: G \rightarrow G/H \) defined by \( \varphi(x) = xH \) for all \( x \in G \) is called the natural projection or canonical homomorphism. It is a surjective homomorphism with kernel \( H \).
A set is numerable (or countably infinite) if its elements can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers ${\mathbb{N}}$. This means that the set can be listed in a sequence.
Non-isomorphic groups are groups that cannot be transformed into each other via an isomorphism, meaning they have different structures despite possibly having the same order.
The symmetric group ${\mathfrak{S}}_{n}$ is the group of all permutations of $n$ elements, serving as a fundamental example in group theory.
Landau's theorem states that for every positive integer $n$, there exists only a finite number of finite non-isomorphic groups that contain exactly $n$ conjugacy classes of elements.
A group admits a cube map if the function $\varphi(x) = x^3$ is a homomorphism, meaning that $(xy)^3 = x^3y^3$ for all elements $x$ and $y$ in the group.
A subgroup $H$ of a group $G$ is termed *nearly normal* if it has finite index in its normal closure.
A technique for efficient exponentiation in a semigroup, allowing the computation of \( a^n \) using \( O(\log_2 n) \) multiplications.
The number of multiplications required to compute \( a^n \) using the method of repeated squaring, specifically \( O(\log_2 n) \).

The kernel $\ker{(f)}$ of any group homomorphism $f: G \longrightarrow G'$ is a normal subgroup of $G$. Furthermore, any normal subgroup $H \subset G$ can be represented as the kernel of some homomorphism, such as the projection map $\rho: G \longrightarrow G/H$, where $G/H$ is the quotient group.
$N_{G}(H)$ is always a subgroup of $G$ and contains $H$, and $H$ is a normal subgroup of $N_{G}(H)$. Furthermore, $H$ is normal in $G$ if and only if $N_{G}(H) = G$.

Concept: Order of an element in a group
Explanation: The order of an element $a$ in a group $G$, denoted $|a|$, is the smallest positive integer $k$ such that $a^k = e_G$, where $e_G$ is the identity element of the group.
Two elements $a$ and $b$ in a group $G$ are said to commute if $ab = ba$. This property is crucial in the proofs of the theorems presented.
A subsemigroup $N$ of a semigroup $S$ is left-normal if $gN \subseteq Ng$ for all $g \in S$.
A subsemigroup $N$ of a semigroup $S$ is right-normal if $gN \supseteq Ng$ for all $g \in S$.
The left-normalizer of a subsemigroup $N$ in a semigroup $S$ is defined as $LN_{S}(N) = \{g \in S \mid gN \subseteq Ng\}$.
The right-normalizer of a subsemigroup $N$ in a semigroup $S$ is defined as $RN_{S}(N) = \{g \in S \mid Ng \subseteq gN\}$.

Concept: Example of left-normal and not right-normal
Explanation: An example is given using matrices where $N$ is left-normal but not right-normal, demonstrating the concepts with specific matrix forms.

A presentation of a group $G$ is a description of $G$ in terms of generators and relations. It is denoted as $G = \langle g_{i} \mid r_{j} \rangle$, where $g_{i}$ are the generators and $r_{j}$ are the defining relations.
A group is finitely presented if it can be described using a finite number of generators and a finite number of defining relations.
A collection of group elements ${g_{i} \in G}$ generates $G$ if every element of $G$ can be expressed as a product of the $g_{i}$ and their inverses.

A relation is a word formed from the generators $g_{i}$ and their inverses that equals the identity in the group $G$. A set of relations is defining if all relations in $G$ can be expressed as products of these.
The Nielsen-Schreier Theorem states that the kernel $N$ in the short exact sequence associated with a group presentation is itself a free group.
A finite symmetric group can be classified as a Coxeter group based on its finite presentation involving generators and relations.
The third isomorphism theorem states that if $K \subseteq H$ are normal subgroups of a group $G$, then $G/H \cong (G/K)/(H/K)$, where $\cong$ denotes isomorphism.
Natural homomorphisms $p: G \rightarrow G/H$ and $q: G \rightarrow G/K$ map elements of $G$ to their respective cosets in the quotient groups.
The trivial group is the group that contains only the identity element, denoted as ${\{ e_{G}\}}$. It is cyclic since it can be generated by its identity element.
A group action of a group $G$ on a set $X$ is called regular if for any pair ${\alpha,\beta} \in X$, there exists exactly one $g \in G$ such that ${g \cdot \alpha} = \beta$.
For a nonempty subset $S$ of a group $G$ and an element $g$ in $G$, the conjugate of $S$ is defined as $gSg^{-1} = \{ gsg^{-1} \mid s \in S \}$.
The group of units in the ring ${\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}}$, consisting of all integers that are coprime to $n$ under multiplication modulo $n$.
The cyclic group of order $n$, which is isomorphic to ${\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}}$.
The set $\{ e \}$, containing only the identity element, is a subgroup of any group.
A subgroup must satisfy the group axioms: closure under the operation, existence of an identity element, and existence of inverses.
A nonempty subset $K$ of a group $G$ is a subgroup if and only if for all $s,t \in K$, the element $st^{-1} \in K$.
The set $({2\mathbb{Z}}, +)$ is a subgroup of $({\mathbb{Z}}, +)$ as it is closed under addition, contains the identity $0$, and has inverses.
A finite cyclic group $G$ is a group that can be generated by a single element and has a finite number of elements, denoted by its order $n$.
A generator of a group $G$ is an element $g$ such that every element of $G$ can be expressed as a power of $g$. In a finite cyclic group, the group can be generated by a single element.
The derived subgroup (or commutator subgroup) $[G,G]$ is the subgroup generated by all commutators in a group $G$. It captures the 'non-abelian' nature of the group.
A function from the empty set to any set exists uniquely as the empty set of ordered pairs, satisfying the definition of a function.

For a set $S$ and a positive integer $n$, $S^{n}$ represents the set of all functions from $\{1, \ldots, n\}$ to $S$. Specifically, $S^{0}$ is the set of all functions from the empty set to $S$, which contains exactly one element.
In category theory, the empty set serves as an initial object, meaning there is a unique morphism from the empty set to any object in the category.

The determinant function \( \det: GL(n,F) \rightarrow F^{\times} \) is a group homomorphism where the kernel is the special linear group \( SL(n,F) \), consisting of matrices with determinant equal to 1.
For any group homomorphism $\phi: G \rightarrow K$, it holds that $\phi(1_G) = 1_K$, where $1_G$ and $1_K$ are the identity elements of groups $G$ and $K$, respectively.

In group theory, the inverse of an element $x$ in a group $G$ is an element $x^{-1}$ such that $x \ast x^{-1} = 1_G$, where $1_G$ is the identity element of the group. The uniqueness of inverses is a fundamental property of groups.

A Cayley table for a group is a matrix that represents the group operation, where the rows and columns are labeled with the group elements, and each cell contains the result of applying the group operation to the corresponding elements.
A family of groups $\mathcal{G} = \{ G_{k} \}_{k \in I}$ is full if for any distinct indices $i,j \in I$, every homomorphism $f: G_{i} \rightarrow G_{j}$ is trivial.
A group is periodic if every element has finite order, meaning for every element $g$ in the group, there exists a positive integer $n$ such that $g^n = e$, where $e$ is the identity element.

The direct product of a family of homomorphisms ${\{ f_{i}:\left. G_{i}\rightarrow H_{i} \right.\}}_{i \in I}$ is defined as a homomorphism ${\prod\limits_{i \in I}f_{i}}:\left. {\prod\limits_{i \in I}G_{i}}\rightarrow{\prod\limits_{i \in I}H_{i}} \right.$, where for each $g \in {\prod_{i \in I}G_{i}}$ and $j \in I$, it holds that ${\left( {\prod\limits_{i \in I}f_{i}} \right)\left( g \right){(j)}} = {f_{j}{({g{(j)}})}}$.

A homomorphism $$f: \underset{i \in I}{\oplus}G_{i} \rightarrow \underset{i \in I}{\oplus}H_{i}$$ is decomposable if there exists a family of homomorphisms $$\{ f_{i}: G_{i} \rightarrow H_{i} \}_{i \in I}$$ such that $$f = \underset{i \in I}{\oplus}f_{i}$$.
An infinite group is a group that has an infinite number of elements. Some infinite groups may have elements of finite order but may not have an exponent, such as $G = \mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$.
The field $\mathbb{F}_{2}$ consists of two elements, typically denoted 0 and 1. The ring of polynomials $\mathbb{F}_{2}[x]$ is an infinite abelian group under addition, with an exponent of 2.
A group is called Hopfian if every surjective homomorphism from the group to itself is an isomorphism.
A group is called co-Hopfian if every injective homomorphism from the group to itself is an isomorphism.

The direct sum of a family of groups is a group formed from the elements of the groups in the family, where each element is a tuple consisting of elements from each group, with all but finitely many entries being the identity element.
If $f$ and $g$ are invertible functions, then their composition $f \circ g$ is also invertible, and its inverse is given by $({f \circ g})^{-1} = g^{-1} \circ f^{-1}$.
This theorem provides an intuitive way to remember that to find the inverse of a composition of functions, the original functions must be undone in the opposite order.
Function composition is associative, meaning that for any functions $f$, $g$, and $h$, we have $(f \circ g) \circ h = f \circ (g \circ h)$.

Concept: Generalization of the Socks and Shoes Rule
Explanation: For a positive integer $n$, if $f_{1}, \ldots, f_{n}$ are invertible functions, then their composition $f_{1} \circ \ldots \circ f_{n}$ is invertible and its inverse is given by $(f_{1} \circ \ldots \circ f_{n})^{-1} = f_{n}^{-1} \circ \ldots \circ f_{1}^{-1}$.
A set of integers is said to be pairwise relatively prime if the greatest common divisor of any two distinct integers in the set is 1.

The generalized quaternion group is defined by the presentation $$Q_{4n} = \langle a,b:a^{n} = b^{2},a^{2n} = 1,b^{- 1}ab = a^{- 1}\rangle$$, where $n > 1$. For $n = 1$, it is isomorphic to the cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_{4}$.
$Q_{4n}$ is Hamiltonian (a non-abelian group whose subgroups are normal) if and only if $n = 2$.
$Q_{4n}$ has a unique minimal subgroup if and only if $n = 2^{i}$.
A proper subgroup $H$ of a group $G$ is defined such that $H \neq G$.
The only subgroup of $G$ that is not a proper subgroup is $G$ itself, which is referred to as an improper subgroup.

A subset $A$ of a group $G$ is symmetric if $A = A^{-1}$, meaning that for every element $a$ in $A$, its inverse $a^{-1}$ is also in $A$. In a vector space, $A$ is symmetric if $A = \{-a : a \in A\}$.

A noncommutative group is a group in which the group operation is not commutative; that is, there exist elements $a$ and $b$ such that $ab \neq ba$. The quaternion group is an example of such a group.

In the context of the quaternion group, the elements $i$, $j$, and $k$ are referred to as imaginary units, analogous to the imaginary unit $i$ in the complex numbers, satisfying $i^{2} = j^{2} = k^{2} = -1$.
Two permutations $\sigma, 	au \in S_{n}$ are conjugate if there exists a permutation $\sigma_{1}$ such that $	au = \sigma_{1} \sigma \sigma_{1}^{-1}$; they have the same cycle type.

The size of the conjugacy class of a permutation $\sigma$ in $S_{n}$ is given by the formula $\frac{n!}{(k_{1}! m_{1}^{k_{1}})(k_{2}! m_{2}^{k_{2}}) \cdots (k_{r}! m_{r}^{k_{r}})}$, where $m_{i}$ are the distinct cycle lengths and $k_{i}$ are the counts of cycles of those lengths.
A *block* is a subset $B$ of a set $A$ such that for each permutation $\sigma \in G$, either $\sigma \cdot B = B$ or $\sigma \cdot B \cap B = \varnothing$.
The trivial blocks are $\varnothing$, $A$, and every element of $A$.
If $B_{1}, B_{2} \subset A$ are blocks of $G$, then $B = B_{1} \cap B_{2}$ is a block of $G$.
If $B$ is a block of $G$ and $\sigma \in G$, then $B$ and $\sigma \cdot B$ are *conjugate blocks*.
The set of all blocks conjugate to a given block is called a *block system*.
The order of a group $G$ is divisible by the size of the set $A$ when $G$ acts transitively on $A$.
The identity permutation is represented as $()$ or $(1)$ and corresponds to a 1-element cycle, which does not change any elements of the set.
Orbits of a permutation $\pi$ are subsets of $S$ where each element is mapped to others by $\pi$. Each orbit with more than 1 element contributes to the cycle structure of the permutation.

A line segment $L$ in a vector space $V$ is defined as $L = \{a + tb \mid t \in [0,1]\}$ for some $a,b \in V$ with $b \neq 0$. It can also be parametrized as $L = \{a + tb \mid t \in (0,1)\}$ for an open line segment.
A closed line segment is defined as $L = \{a + tb \mid t \in [0,1]\}$, which includes the endpoints.
An open line segment is defined as $L = \{a + tb \mid t \in (0,1)\}$, which excludes the endpoints.
A closed line segment can also be described as the convex hull of two distinct points $x$ and $y$, denoted as $[x,y] = \{\alpha x + (1 - \alpha)y \mid 0 \leq \alpha \leq 1\}$.
A line segment is a connected, non-empty set in the context of topology.
The symmetric group $S_k$ is the group of all permutations of $k$ elements, with the group operation being the composition of permutations.
The alternating group $A_k$ is the group of all even permutations of $k$ elements, which is a normal subgroup of the symmetric group $S_k$.

A group \( G \) acting on a set \( X \) is called doubly transitive if for any two pairs of distinct elements \( (y_1, y_2) \) and \( (x_1, x_2) \) in \( X \), there exists an element \( \sigma \in G \) such that \( \sigma \cdot y_1 = x_1 \) and \( \sigma \cdot y_2 = x_2 \).
An action of a group \( G \) on a set \( X \) is called primitive if it preserves no non-trivial blocks, meaning the only blocks are singletons or the entire set \( X \).
A group is doubly transitive if it acts transitively on pairs of distinct elements. ${\mathcal{D}_{2n}}$ is not doubly transitive for $n \geq 4$.
A group is primitive if it acts transitively on a set without any nontrivial blocks. For prime $p$, ${\mathcal{D}_{2p}}$ is shown to be primitive.

A transitive permutation group $G$ on a set $X$ is called a *primitive permutation group* if it has no nontrivial blocks. This means that there are no partitions of $X$ into subsets that are invariant under the action of $G$ other than the trivial partitions.

A permutation group $G$ on a set $X$ is transitive if for any two elements $x, y \in X$, there exists a group element $g \in G$ such that $g \cdot x = y$. This property indicates that the group acts uniformly across the set.

A subgroup $H$ of a group $G$ is called a maximal subgroup if there are no other subgroups $K$ such that $H < K < G$. In the context of primitive permutation groups, the stabilizer ${Stab}_{G}(x)$ being a maximal subgroup for all $x \in X$ is a necessary and sufficient condition for $G$ to be primitive.

A group action is primitive if it has no nontrivial blocks, meaning the only blocks are singletons or the entire set $A$. This implies that the only subgroups containing the stabilizer $G_a$ are $G_a$ itself and $G$.

$PGL(V)$ is not 3-transitive on the set of all points in $PG(V)$ if $\dim V 
eq 2$, indicating that it cannot map any ordered triple of distinct points to any other ordered triple in those cases.

Jordan's theorem states that if $G$ is a sharply $n$-transitive permutation group with $n \geq 4$, then $G$ is similar to either the symmetric group $S_n$, the alternating group $A_{n+2}$, or specific Mathieu groups $M_{11}$ and $M_{12}$ for $n=4$ and $n=5$, respectively.
A group $G$ is $n$-transitive on a set $X$ if it can map any $n$ distinct elements of $X$ to any other $n$ distinct elements of $X$ via its permutations.
The stabilizer subgroup $G_{x}$ is the set of permutations in $G$ that fix the element $x$ while permuting the other elements in $X$.
A group is *transitive* if it can map any single element of the set to any other element of the set.
The alternating group $A_n$ is the group of even permutations of $n$ elements, which is $(n-2)$-transitive.
The symmetric group $S_n$ is the group of all permutations of $n$ elements, which is $n$-transitive.
An action of a group $G$ on a set $X$ is called $n$-transitive if it acts transitively on the set of $n$-tuples of distinct elements of $X$, denoted $X^{(n)}$.

The Mathieu groups are a set of 5 sporadic simple groups denoted by $M_{11}$, $M_{12}$, $M_{22}$, $M_{23}$, $M_{24}$, discovered by Émile Léonard Mathieu. They are defined as automorphism groups of certain Steiner systems.
A unique Steiner system denoted by $S(5,8,24)$, constructed from the octads of the extended binary Golay Code $\mathcal{G}_{24}$.
A unique Steiner system denoted by $S(5,6,12)$, constructed from subsets of size 6 in a dodecad of the extended binary Golay Code $\mathcal{G}_{24}$.
An ordering \( \leq \) (or \( < \)) of a set \( A \) is called *linear* or *total* if any two elements of \( A \) are comparable. The pair \( (A, \leq) \) is then called a *linearly ordered set*.
An action of a group $G$ on a set $X$ is called sharply $n$-transitive if $G$ acts regularly on the set of ordered $n$-tuples of distinct elements of $X$, denoted as $X^{(n)}$.

The MOG is a construction of the \[24,12,8\] extended binary Golay code \(\mathcal{G}_{24}\), utilizing the hexacode and represented as a rectangular array of 4 rows and 6 columns with specific parity and sum conditions.
The hexacode is a code used in the conditions for forming subsets \(S\) in the MOG, where the sums of the row labels for elements of \(S\) in each column must form an element of the hexacode.

A mathematical structure that studies the properties of geometric figures that remain invariant under projective transformations. It underlies the symmetry properties of patterns in the context of the Cullinane diamond theorem.

Groups that describe the symmetries of a mathematical structure, where each group element corresponds to a symmetry operation. The theory of automorphism groups of finite geometries is used to explain the interrelationships and symmetries of the patterns in the diamond theorem.
A group of transformations that includes linear transformations and translations. In this context, the group G is isomorphic to the affine group A on the linear 4-space over GF(2).

A finite projective space consisting of points and lines, where the lines are defined as sets of points. The 35 structures of the diamond theorem correspond to the 35 three-point lines of this projective space.

A property related to the arrangement of symbols in a square grid such that each symbol appears exactly once in each row and column. In the diamond theorem, orthogonality corresponds to the skewness of lines in the finite projective space PG(3,2).
A combinatorial design that arranges a set of elements into subsets with specific intersection properties. The underlying geometry of the diamond patterns is related to this system.
The symmetric group on 4 letters, which has 24 elements and represents all possible permutations of a set of 4 elements.
A subgroup that does not act transitively on a set, resulting in multiple orbits.
A maximal p-subgroup of a group, where p is a prime dividing the order of the group.
The group of symmetries of a square, including rotations and reflections, which has 8 elements.
The group of even permutations of four elements, which is a normal subgroup of S_{4} of order 12.
The Klein four-group, which is an abelian group of order 4, isomorphic to the direct product of two copies of the cyclic group of order 2.

The order of a group $G$, denoted $|G|$, is the number of elements in $G$. For a subgroup $H = \langle S \rangle$, its order can be determined by examining the subgroup chain and the indices of the subgroups.

The membership test determines if an element $g \in G$ belongs to a subgroup $H = \langle S \rangle$. This can be approached by checking the equality of orders of the groups generated by $S$ and $S,g$.
A subgroup chain is a sequence of subgroups $H = H_k > H_{k-1} > \cdots > H_0 = 1$ that illustrates the structure of a group and aids in computing its order.
Transversals are representatives of the cosets of a subgroup in a group. They are used to compute the order of a group by considering the indices of subgroups in a chain.
Schreier's lemma provides a method to construct transversals in a chain of subgroups, which is essential for solving problems related to group order and membership in permutation groups.
A polycyclic presentation is a way of describing a group that exhibits a chain of subgroups with known prime indices, facilitating the computation of group order and membership testing.
General presentations of groups can be complex, and determining properties such as whether a group is trivial can be undecidable, complicating membership testing.
A group is simple if it has no nontrivial normal subgroups other than itself and the trivial group.
A 3D combination puzzle consisting of smaller cubes that can be rotated around its axes, where the goal is to align all faces to show a single color.
A method for solving the Rubik's Cube by completing one layer at a time, typically starting from the top layer and working downwards.
A move that transfers an edge piece from the bottom layer to the middle layer of the Rubik's Cube.
A move that changes the position of two corner pieces in the bottom layer of the Rubik's Cube.
A move that changes the orientation of three corner pieces in the bottom layer of the Rubik's Cube.
A move that changes the position of edge pieces in the bottom layer of the Rubik's Cube, with left and right variants.
The stabilizer of any point in $X$ is called a Frobenius complement, which is distinct from any conjugate by an element not in the subgroup.

A permutation $	au \in S(p + q)$ is a $(p,q)$ shuffle if the first $p$ elements are less than the next $q$ elements, specifically $	au(1) < \cdots < 	au(p)$ and $	au(p + 1) < \cdots < 	au(p + q)$. The set of all $(p,q)$ shuffles is denoted by $S(p,q)$.
The cardinality of the set of $(p,q)$ shuffles, $S(p,q)$, is given by $\binom{p + q}{p}$, which counts the number of ways to choose $p$ positions from $p + q$ total positions.

A permutation $\tau \in S(p + q)$ is a $(p,q)$ unshuffle if there exist indices $i_{1} < \cdots < i_{p}$ and $j_{1} < \cdots < j_{q}$ such that $\tau(i_{k}) = k$ for $k = 1, \ldots, p$ and $\tau(j_{l}) = p + l$ for $l = 1, \ldots, q$. Alternatively, $\tau$ is a $(p,q)$ unshuffle if $\tau^{-1}$ is a $(p,q)$ shuffle.
$S(k)$ denotes the symmetric group on the set of numbers $\{1, \ldots, k\}$, which consists of all possible permutations of these numbers.
The cardinality of the set of $(p,q)$ unshuffles is given by the binomial coefficient $\binom{p + q}{q}$, which counts the number of ways to choose $q$ elements from a set of $p + q$ elements.
When $K$ is a field, the group ring $K{\lbrack G\rbrack}$ is referred to as a group algebra, which is constructed similarly to a group ring but over a field.
Two subgroups $S_{1}, S_{2} \subseteq G$ are commensurable if their intersection $S_{1} \cap S_{2}$ has finite index in both $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$, denoted as $S_{1} \sim S_{2}$.
All non-zero subgroups of $\mathbb{Z}$ and all conjugacy classes of $GL(n; \mathbb{Z})$ as a subgroup of $GL(n; \mathbb{Q})$ are examples of commensurable subgroups.
A linear transformation $T$ is onto if the column space of the matrix $A$, denoted as $Col A$, spans the entire target space $\mathbb{R}^{m}$.
A linear transformation is one-to-one if the corresponding homogeneous system has only the trivial solution, meaning that $T(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ implies $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$.

The first orthogonality relation states that for irreducible representations $\rho_{\alpha}$ and $\rho_{\beta}$ of a finite group $G$, the inner product of their characters satisfies $$\frac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g \in G} \overline{\rho_{ij}^{(\alpha)}(g)} \rho_{kl}^{(\beta)}(g) = \frac{\delta_{\alpha\beta}\delta_{ik}\delta_{jl}}{\dim V_{\alpha}}.$$

The characters $\chi_1$ and $\chi_2$ of representations $V_1$ and $V_2$ of a finite group $G$ are related by the inner product $$ (\chi_1, \chi_2) = \frac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g \in G} \overline{\chi_1(g)}\chi_2(g) = \dim(Hom(V_1, V_2)). $$

Schur's Lemma states that for irreducible representations $V_1$ and $V_2$, the homomorphism space is given by $$Hom(V_1, V_2) = \begin{cases} D & V_1 \cong V_2 \ 0 & V_1 \ncong V_2 \end{cases}$$ where $D$ is a division algebra.

The multiplicities $n_i$ in the decomposition of a representation $V$ into irreducibles can be computed using the character inner product: $$n_i = \frac{(\psi, \chi_i)}{(\chi_i, \chi_i)}$$ where $\psi$ is the character of $V$ and $\chi_i$ the character of $V_i$.
